patterns.title: Bottom microphone thermal sensor failure (mic1)
patterns.diagnosis: The mic1 NTC thermal sensor on the lightning/charging flex cable is disconnected or damaged.

probabilities.description: The NTC sensor is soldered on the flex. Flex replacement resolves.
Codec IC (338S00509) not reading sensor. Requires micro-soldering.
Standalone NTC failure. Replaceable with micro-soldering.

repair_steps: 1. Replace lightning/charging flex cable assembly
2. Test bottom microphone in Voice Memos (record + playback)
3. Verify thermal sensor reads in diagnostics
4. If persists: inspect codec IC under microscope
test_procedure: Voice Memos: record 10s, play back — should hear clear audio from bottom mic
Phone call: speaker on mute, other party should hear you clearly
Diagnostics: Settings > Privacy > Analytics > check for new panic-full logs

patterns.title: Side/rear microphone thermal sensor failure (mic2)
patterns.diagnosis: The mic2 NTC thermal sensor (near volume buttons or rear mic) is disconnected.

probabilities.description: mic2 sensor on the rear camera or side flex. Replace flex.
Connector dislodged during previous repair.
Trace to mic2 sensor broken. Requires micro-soldering.

repair_steps: 1. Reseat or replace rear camera/mic flex cable
2. Check FPC connector on logic board under microscope
3. Test all microphones (Voice Memos, speakerphone, Siri)
4. If persists: board-level trace repair
test_procedure: Voice Memos: record with phone facing away (rear mic test)
Video recording: check audio clarity
Monitor Analytics for new panic-full logs after repair

patterns.title: Top microphone thermal sensor failure (mic3)
patterns.diagnosis: The mic3 NTC thermal sensor near the earpiece/top speaker is disconnected.

probabilities.description: mic3 is on the earpiece/front sensor flex. Replace flex.
Flex damaged during screen replacement. Reseat or replace.
FPC connector damaged. Micro-soldering required.

repair_steps: 1. Replace earpiece/proximity sensor flex cable
2. Verify all front sensors reconnected properly
3. Test top microphone (Siri, phone calls)
4. If persists: inspect FPC connector on board
test_procedure: Siri activation and voice recognition test
Phone call quality (earpiece mic)
Check for new panic logs after 24h

patterns.title: Ambient light sensor failure (ALS)
patterns.diagnosis: The ambient light sensor on the front flex cable is disconnected or damaged. Auto-brightness will not work.

probabilities.description: ALS is on the proximity/earpiece flex. Replace flex.
Flex not transferred or damaged during screen swap.
ALS controller IC failed. Micro-soldering required.

repair_steps: 1. Replace proximity/earpiece sensor flex cable
2. If aftermarket screen: ensure ALS flex is transferred from original
3. Test auto-brightness in Settings > Display
4. If persists: ALS IC on board (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Auto-brightness: cover sensor, screen should dim
Settings > Accessibility > Display > Auto-Brightness toggle
Monitor for new panic-full logs

patterns.title: Proximity sensor failure
patterns.diagnosis: The proximity sensor is disconnected. Screen stays on during calls (burns ear, pocket dials).

probabilities.description: Proximity sensor on the earpiece/front flex. Replace flex.
Aftermarket screen without proximity flex. Transfer from OEM.
FPC connector pins bent or corroded.

repair_steps: 1. Replace or reseat proximity/earpiece flex cable
2. For aftermarket screens: transfer OEM proximity flex
3. Test proximity during phone call (screen should turn off near ear)
4. If persists: inspect board FPC connector
test_procedure: Phone call: hold phone to ear, screen should turn off
Cover proximity sensor with finger — screen should blank
Check Settings > Phone > Proximity Sensor behavior

patterns.title: Barometer/pressure sensor failure (PRS0)
patterns.diagnosis: The PRS0 barometric pressure sensor is disconnected. Located on the charging port flex cable.

probabilities.description: PRS0 is on the lightning flex. Replace the entire flex.
Barometer IC failed. Usually resolves with flex replacement.
Trace to barometer broken on board.

repair_steps: 1. Replace lightning/charging port flex cable assembly
2. Test barometer: Compass app > elevation reading
3. Monitor for panic recurrence over 24h
test_procedure: Compass app: check if elevation/altitude is displayed
Weather app: verify local pressure reading
Health app: check flights climbed tracking

patterns.title: Battery temperature sensor failure (TG0B)
patterns.diagnosis: The battery NTC temperature sensor is disconnected or damaged. Device may refuse to charge.

probabilities.description: Battery flex not properly connected. Reseat connector.
NTC on battery flex is damaged. Replace battery.
Battery FPC connector pins damaged.

repair_steps: 1. Disconnect and reseat battery connector firmly
2. If persists: replace battery
3. Inspect board battery connector under microscope
4. If connector damaged: micro-soldering required
test_procedure: Check battery health: Settings > Battery > Battery Health
Verify charging works (both wired and wireless if supported)
Monitor temperature during use (should not overheat)

patterns.title: Battery voltage/fuel gauge sensor failure (TG0V)
patterns.diagnosis: The battery fuel gauge IC (bq27541) cannot communicate. Battery percentage may be inaccurate.

probabilities.description: Battery flex not seated. Reseat connector.
bq27541 chip on battery failed. Replace battery.
Communication line to fuel gauge broken on board.

repair_steps: 1. Disconnect and reseat battery connector
2. Replace battery if reseating doesn't help
3. Check gas gauge communication lines on board (advanced)
test_procedure: Check battery percentage accuracy (charge to 100%, use, verify drain is linear)
coconutBattery or 3uTools battery health check
Verify no unexpected shutdowns at >20% battery

patterns.title: Thermal monitoring service failure
patterns.diagnosis: thermalmonitord crashed or hung, usually due to a missing/disconnected thermal sensor.

probabilities.description: A sensor cable is damaged or not seated. Check panic string for 'Missing sensor' to identify which.
PMU or thermal controller IC fault.
Rare. Check if specific to one iOS version.

repair_steps: 1. Identify which sensor is missing from panic string
2. Map sensor to physical component (mic1=lightning flex, als=proximity flex, etc.)
3. Replace the corresponding flex cable
4. If no sensor identified: check all flex connections, reseat
test_procedure: Monitor for recurrence over 24h after repair
Check Analytics for new crash logs

patterns.title: GPU firmware lockup / hang
patterns.diagnosis: The GPU core locked up, triggering a firmware reset or kernel panic.

probabilities.description: Cold solder joint under SoC. Common after drop or thermal cycling.
GPU thermal paste dried, heatsink not seated.
Check if specific to one iOS version. Try DFU restore.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore to rule out software
2. If persists: inspect board for signs of heat damage near SoC
3. Reball SoC (advanced micro-soldering, low success rate)
4. Board swap may be necessary
test_procedure: Run a graphics-intensive app (game or benchmark)
Monitor for GPU recovery messages in console
Check for visual artifacts on screen

patterns.title: Kernel data abort — invalid memory access
patterns.diagnosis: The kernel accessed an invalid memory address, causing an immediate panic.

probabilities.description: LPDDR4/5 cell failure. Not repairable.
Flash storage read error propagated to kernel. Try DFU restore first.
Rare. Check if tied to specific iOS version.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore (rule out NAND filesystem corruption)
2. If persists: board-level diagnosis required
3. RAM is part of SoC package — not field-repairable
4. Board swap
test_procedure: After DFU restore, use phone normally for 48h
Run memory-intensive apps
Check Analytics for recurrence

patterns.title: Display/touch service timeout (backboardd)
patterns.diagnosis: backboardd (display + touch input service) stopped responding.

probabilities.description: Damaged or poorly seated display flex. Check FPC connector.
Connector pins bent or corroded on logic board.
Touch controller IC failure. Requires micro-soldering.

repair_steps: 1. Reseat display flex cable
2. Inspect FPC connector under microscope (bent/missing pins)
3. Try known-good display
4. If persists with good display: Meson IC or board issue
test_procedure: Touch responsiveness test (draw app, all screen areas)
3D Touch / Haptic Touch pressure test
Display color/dead pixel check

patterns.title: WiFi/Bluetooth service failure (wifid)
patterns.diagnosis: wifid stopped responding, indicating WiFi/BT chip communication failure.

probabilities.description: USI 339S00648 or similar. Common after water damage.
WiFi antenna cable not seated or damaged.
Try network settings reset, then DFU restore.

repair_steps: 1. Reset network settings
2. DFU restore
3. Check antenna connections
4. If persists: WiFi IC replacement (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: WiFi: connect to 2.4GHz and 5GHz networks, speed test
Bluetooth: pair AirPods or speaker
AirDrop: test file transfer
Check Settings > General > About > WiFi Address (if 'N/A' = chip dead)

patterns.title: Media services failure (mediaserverd)
patterns.diagnosis: mediaserverd hung — handles audio/video playback. Often related to audio IC or codec.

probabilities.description: Audio codec IC unresponsive. Common on iPhone 7/7+ (audio IC disease).
Audio output flex not seated. Reseat connections.
Try DFU restore first.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore
2. Reseat speaker and earpiece flex cables
3. Test with known-good speaker assembly
4. If persists: audio IC replacement (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Play music through speaker
Phone call: test earpiece audio
Voice Memos: record and playback
Check if Voice Memos app opens without freezing

patterns.title: Location service failure (locationd)
patterns.diagnosis: locationd hung — handles GPS/location. May indicate GPS antenna or baseband issue.

probabilities.description: GPS antenna cable not seated. Check connections.
GPS is handled by baseband IC. If no signal either, baseband fault.
Reset location services, try DFU restore.

repair_steps: 1. Reset Location & Privacy settings
2. DFU restore
3. Check GPS antenna cable connections
4. If persists with no cellular signal: baseband IC issue
test_procedure: Open Maps app — does location pin appear?
Compass app — does heading work?
Check cellular signal simultaneously

patterns.title: SpringBoard (home screen) failure
patterns.diagnosis: SpringBoard stopped responding. Usually software-related but can indicate memory issues.

probabilities.description: Filesystem or app cache corruption. DFU restore usually fixes.
Device nearly full, SpringBoard can't cache. Free up space.
If frequent after DFU restore, may indicate RAM failure.

repair_steps: 1. Force restart (volume up, volume down, hold power)
2. Free storage space if nearly full
3. DFU restore
4. If persists after restore: suspect RAM (board swap)
test_procedure: Normal usage for 24h after restore
Open 10+ apps rapidly, switch between them
Check storage: Settings > General > iPhone Storage

patterns.title: Secure Enclave Processor panic
patterns.diagnosis: The SEP crashed. This is a critical, board-level issue.

probabilities.description: SEP is part of the SoC. Not field-repairable.
Power rail issue affecting SEP.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore (very unlikely to help but worth trying)
2. Board swap — SEP is fused to SoC, cannot be replaced independently
3. Device may be unrepairable if paired components are involved
test_procedure: Face ID / Touch ID test
Apple Pay enrollment test
Keychain access test

patterns.title: Memory pressure — app killed by Jetsam
patterns.diagnosis: iOS killed a process due to memory pressure. Usually normal behavior under heavy multitasking.

probabilities.description: Heavy app usage, many background processes. Not a hardware issue.
A specific app consuming excessive memory.
If Jetsam events are extremely frequent (100+/day), may indicate bad RAM cells.

repair_steps: 1. Check frequency — occasional Jetsam is normal
2. If excessive: identify the largest process in the log
3. Force-close heavy apps, restart device
4. If 100+ events/day: DFU restore, then suspect RAM if persists
test_procedure: Monitor Jetsam frequency over 24h
Note which apps trigger the most events
Compare to baseline (5-20/day is normal under active use)

patterns.title: Baseband/cellular modem crash
patterns.diagnosis: The cellular modem firmware crashed. Device may show 'No Service' or 'Searching'.

probabilities.description: Qualcomm/Intel modem IC soldering issue or failure.
Baseband power management IC not supplying correct voltage.
Carrier settings or iOS baseband firmware corruption.

repair_steps: 1. Check for 'No Service' or missing IMEI (Settings > General > About)
2. Reset network settings
3. DFU restore with latest iOS
4. If IMEI missing: baseband IC or BBPMU replacement (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Check IMEI in Settings > General > About (if 'Unknown' = baseband dead)
Insert known-good SIM, check for cellular signal
Make a phone call
Check if airplane mode toggle works

patterns.title: NAND storage controller panic (ANS2)
patterns.diagnosis: The Apple NAND Storage v2 controller lost communication with flash storage. Critical storage issue.

probabilities.description: Flash storage IC failing. May need NAND replacement.
SoC-NAND communication line fault. Board-level issue.
Severe corruption. DFU restore may recover.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore (may fail if NAND is physically damaged)
2. If restore fails: NAND chip replacement (expert micro-soldering)
3. If NAND replaced: reprogram with original serial numbers
4. Board swap if unrepairable
test_procedure: DFU restore attempt — does it complete?
After restore: write large file, verify data integrity
Check storage capacity: Settings > General > iPhone Storage
Monitor for boot loops

patterns.title: SMC panic assertion failure (iPhone 13+)
patterns.diagnosis: System Management Controller assertion failed. Common on iPhone 13+ when sensor arrays report faults.

probabilities.description: One or more sensor flex cables not seated. Check all connections.
Screen replacement missing sensor data. Use OEM or quality aftermarket with sensor transfer.
SMC IC or power rail fault. Advanced repair.

repair_steps: 1. Check all flex cable connections (display, battery, camera, charging)
2. If aftermarket screen: transfer OEM sensor flex or use compatible screen
3. Decode sensor array code in panic log to identify specific sensor
4. If all connections good: SMC issue, board-level repair
test_procedure: Check all sensors: proximity, ALS, microphones, barometer
Auto-brightness test
Monitor for panic recurrence after reseating all flexes

patterns.title: Sleep/Wake hang — audio codec or power issue
patterns.diagnosis: Device hung during sleep/wake transition. Often related to audio IC, amplifier, or power management.

probabilities.description: Audio codec not responding during sleep transition. iPhone 7 'audio IC disease'.
PMU not handling sleep states properly.
Backlight driver not responding during wake.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore
2. Test audio: Voice Memos, speakerphone, headphones
3. If audio issues present: audio IC replacement (micro-soldering)
4. If no audio issues: check backlight IC and PMU
test_procedure: Lock/unlock phone 20 times rapidly
Lock phone, wait 5 min, unlock — any delay?
Voice Memos recording during sleep/wake cycles

patterns.title: Audio IC / codec failure
patterns.diagnosis: The Cirrus Logic audio codec IC is failing. Known as 'audio IC disease' on iPhone 7/7+.

probabilities.description: Cold solder joints under audio IC. Very common on iPhone 7. Reball required.
Pads lifted from board. Jumper wire repair needed.
IC itself failed. Full IC replacement.

repair_steps: 1. Test: does Voice Memos app open? (frozen = classic audio IC symptom)
2. Test speakerphone during call
3. Reball audio IC (remove, clean pads, reball, resolder)
4. If pads damaged: jumper wire repair to restore connections
test_procedure: Open Voice Memos — does it freeze/crash?
Speakerphone call — does speaker toggle work?
Record video — is there audio in playback?
Headphones: does audio output work via jack/lightning?

patterns.title: Tristar/Hydra USB charging IC failure
patterns.diagnosis: The Tristar (CBTL1612) or Hydra USB controller IC failed. Causes charging issues, 'This accessory may not be supported'.

probabilities.description: Tristar corroded from liquid exposure. IC replacement required.
IC degraded from using non-MFi cables. Replacement needed.
Lightning port dirty or damaged. Try flex replacement first.

repair_steps: 1. Clean lightning port (IPA + brush)
2. Try known-good MFi cable
3. Replace charging port flex cable
4. If persists: Tristar/Hydra IC replacement (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Charge with MFi cable — does it charge?
Check for 'This accessory may not be supported' popup
Connect to iTunes/Finder — does computer recognize device?
Check charging current with USB ammeter

patterns.title: Face ID / TrueDepth camera system failure
patterns.diagnosis: The TrueDepth camera system (dot projector, flood illuminator, or IR camera) has failed.

probabilities.description: Dot projector laser module failed. Not individually replaceable on most models.
IR LED flood illuminator not functioning.
TrueDepth flex cable damaged during screen replacement.
VCSEL driver IC on board failed.

repair_steps: 1. Check if Face ID was working before last repair
2. Reseat front camera/TrueDepth flex cable
3. Inspect TrueDepth module under microscope
4. On iPhone 12+: TrueDepth module can be transferred to new screen
5. If module damaged: Face ID non-functional (no repair, device still usable)
test_procedure: Settings > Face ID & Passcode > Set Up Face ID
Front camera: does Portrait mode work?
Animoji: do face tracking animations work?
Check for 'Face ID is not available' error in Settings

patterns.title: NFC controller failure
patterns.diagnosis: The NFC controller IC (NXP) is not responding. Apple Pay and NFC will not work.

probabilities.description: NXP NFC controller IC failed. Micro-soldering replacement.
NFC antenna coil disconnected or damaged.
DFU restore may resolve.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore
2. Check NFC antenna connections
3. Test Apple Pay enrollment
4. If persists: NFC IC replacement (micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Apple Pay: try adding a card
NFC tag: hold an NFC tag to back of phone
Express Transit: test with transit card if available

patterns.title: Ultra-Wideband (U1/U2) chip failure
patterns.diagnosis: The UWB chip is not responding. AirDrop direction finding and Precision Finding for AirTags won't work.

probabilities.description: U1/U2 chip failed. Not practically repairable.
UWB antenna cable not seated.
DFU restore may resolve.

repair_steps: 1. DFU restore
2. Check UWB antenna connections (near top of device)
3. If persists: UWB chip failure — device still functional without precision finding
test_procedure: AirDrop: test with nearby device — does direction arrow appear?
Find My: test Precision Finding with an AirTag
Nearby Interactions: test with another iPhone 11+

patterns.title: Camera ISP (Image Signal Processor) panic
patterns.diagnosis: The camera ISP crashed. Camera may show black screen or freeze the camera app.

probabilities.description: Camera flex cable damaged. Replace rear camera module.
Camera FPC not properly connected after repair.
ISP is part of SoC. DFU restore may help if software-related.

repair_steps: 1. Reseat rear camera flex cable
2. Try known-good camera module
3. DFU restore
4. If persists with good camera: ISP/SoC issue (board swap)
test_procedure: Open Camera app — does viewfinder show?
Take photo with each camera (wide, ultra-wide, telephoto)
Record video — does it complete without freezing?
Test flashlight toggle

patterns.title: Thermal shutdown — excessive temperature
patterns.diagnosis: Device shut down due to excessive temperature. May indicate cooling system or battery issue.

probabilities.description: Direct sunlight, hot car, charging while gaming. Not a defect.
Old or defective battery causing excess heat. Replace battery.
Component on board drawing excess current, generating heat.
Thermal interface material dried out (older devices).

repair_steps: 1. Check battery health (Settings > Battery > Battery Health)
2. If battery <80%: replace battery
3. Check for board-level shorts with thermal camera or DC power supply
4. If specific area hot: identify and replace faulty component
test_procedure: Use phone normally for 1h — does it get excessively hot?
Charge phone — is charging area extremely hot?
Use thermal camera to identify hot spots on board
Battery health percentage check

patterns.title: Power Management Unit (PMU) failure
patterns.diagnosis: The PMU/PMIC chip is not functioning correctly. Causes boot issues, random shutdowns, or no power.

probabilities.description: PMU chip (Dialog/Apple) failed. Micro-soldering replacement.
Short circuit on a power rail. Identify and fix shorted component.
Battery not providing correct voltage. Try new battery.

repair_steps: 1. Check boot current with DC power supply
2. Measure voltage rails with multimeter
3. Identify shorted rails with thermal camera
4. PMU replacement (advanced micro-soldering)
test_procedure: Does device boot?
Boot current pattern (normal is spike then settle)
Check all voltage rails with multimeter
Random shutdown frequency

patterns.title: Display backlight / brightness IC failure
patterns.diagnosis: The backlight driver IC failed. Screen may appear very dim or have no backlight.

probabilities.description: Backlight driver IC failed. Micro-soldering replacement.
Backlight filter on board blown (often from connecting screen while battery is connected).
Backlight lines in display flex damaged.

repair_steps: 1. Test: shine flashlight at screen — can you see image? (backlight dead but display works)
2. Check backlight filter/fuse on board
3. Replace backlight IC if filter is good
4. Try known-good display to rule out flex damage
test_procedure: Flashlight test: shine light through screen, look for faint image
Auto-brightness toggle test
Maximum brightness slider test
Connect to computer — does iTunes/Finder detect device?

patterns.title: I2C/SPI bus communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: Inter-chip communication bus failed. Multiple sensors/ICs may be affected.

probabilities.description: Corrosion on bus lines. Board cleaning and trace repair.
Drop damage causing broken traces.
A device on the I2C/SPI bus pulling lines low.

repair_steps: 1. Inspect board under microscope for corrosion/damage
2. Clean board with IPA and ultrasonic cleaner
3. Check I2C/SPI lines with oscilloscope
4. Repair broken traces or replace faulty ICs
test_procedure: Check which functions are not working (often multiple)
Test: touch, sensors, camera, WiFi systematically
Look for corrosion indicators (LCI stickers)

patterns.title: App crash — memory access violation (EXC_BAD_ACCESS)
patterns.diagnosis: The app accessed an invalid memory address. Usually a software bug (null pointer, use-after-free), but if it affects many different apps it may indicate RAM issues.

probabilities.description: Most common. Update or reinstall the app.
If many unrelated apps crash with SIGSEGV, suspect soldered RAM failure.
Known iOS bug in specific version. Check for iOS update.

repair_steps: 1. Update or reinstall the crashing app
2. Update iOS to latest version
3. If many different apps affected: backup + restore iPhone
4. If persists after restore: suspect RAM failure — professional board repair needed
test_procedure: Reproduce crash in the specific app
Test same operation in other apps
Check if crash disappears after iOS update

patterns.title: App crash — assertion failure (SIGABRT)
patterns.diagnosis: The app called abort() due to an assertion failure or unhandled exception. This is almost always a software issue in the app itself.

probabilities.cause: App bug — unhandled exception or failed assertion
probabilities.description: Developer error. Update or reinstall the app.
App state corrupted. Offload and reinstall the app.

repair_steps: 1. Update the app to latest version
2. Offload and reinstall the app (Settings > General > iPhone Storage)
3. Contact app developer if issue persists
test_procedure: Check App Store for updates
Reproduce with fresh app install

patterns.title: App terminated — memory limit exceeded
patterns.diagnosis: The app was killed by iOS because it exceeded its memory allocation. Normal for memory-heavy apps; persistent cases may indicate a memory leak.

probabilities.description: The app is consuming too much RAM. Update or reinstall.

probabilities.cause: Device under memory pressure (too many apps open)
probabilities.description: Close background apps and test again.
App requires more RAM than available on this model.

repair_steps: 1. Update the app
2. Close background apps before using it
3. Restart iPhone before testing
4. Offload and reinstall the app
test_procedure: Open app after fresh restart with no background apps
Monitor if crash still occurs

patterns.title: Memory pressure — system killed background apps (Jetsam)
patterns.diagnosis: iOS killed background apps to free RAM. This is normal behavior. Very frequent events may indicate insufficient RAM for the workload or an app with a memory leak.

probabilities.description: iOS kills background apps when foreground apps need RAM. Not a problem.
A specific app is consuming too much RAM. Identify and update it.
Very old iPhones may show degraded RAM performance over time.

repair_steps: 1. Check which app triggers most jetsam events (Largest Process field)
2. Update or reinstall that app
3. Restart iPhone periodically
test_procedure: Note the 'largest process' in the crash details
Test device performance after closing that app

patterns.title: GPU hang — graphics processor lockup
patterns.diagnosis: The GPU locked up and required a forced restart. Isolated events may be software-related; repeated events suggest a hardware GPU or thermal issue.

probabilities.cause: GPU driver bug / app pushing GPU too hard
probabilities.description: Update iOS and the app causing GPU stress.
Device overheating causes GPU errors. Check thermal events.
GPU silicon defect or solder joint failure. Board repair required.

repair_steps: 1. Update iOS to latest version
2. Update the app triggering GPU events
3. Ensure device is not overheating during use
4. If frequent: professional board-level GPU repair
test_procedure: Test GPU-intensive tasks (games, video) after cooling device
Check for thermal events in the same time period

patterns.title: OTA/restore failure — possible hardware issue
patterns.diagnosis: The device failed during an OTA update or restore. Error 4013/4014/9 typically indicate a hardware problem with the dock connector, NAND chip, or logic board.

probabilities.description: Faulty cable or dock connector causing communication failure. Try original cable.
NAND chip or controller failing. Board repair required.
Baseband firmware update failed. Board repair.
Try different USB port, cable, and computer.

repair_steps: 1. Try with original Apple cable on a different USB port
2. Update iTunes/Finder to latest version
3. If Error 4013: inspect/replace dock connector
4. If Error 4014: professional NAND repair/replacement
test_procedure: Verify cable and computer connection
Check error code in OTA log
Attempt DFU restore

patterns.title: Thermal event — device temperature management
patterns.diagnosis: iOS logged a thermal event to manage device temperature. Isolated events are normal during heavy workloads. Frequent events at idle may indicate a hardware thermal issue.

probabilities.description: Expected during gaming, video recording, charging. Not a problem.
Identify the process in thermal logs and update/replace it.

probabilities.cause: Thermal sensor or cooling system fault
probabilities.description: Defective thermal sensor or damaged heat spreader. Hardware repair.

repair_steps: 1. Identify if thermal events occur during specific activities
2. Ensure device is not in a case that traps heat
3. Check for app consuming high CPU in the background
4. If device is hot at idle: suspect hardware thermal sensor issue
test_procedure: Monitor device temperature during idle vs load
Check which process is listed in thermal logs

patterns.title: Watchdog timeout — system daemon did not respond
patterns.diagnosis: A critical iOS system daemon failed to respond within the watchdog timeout, forcing a reboot. The specific daemon indicates which subsystem failed.

probabilities.description: iOS software issue. Update iOS.

probabilities.cause: Hardware component served by that daemon is failing
probabilities.description: If the daemon manages hardware (audio, display, WiFi), the underlying hardware may be failing.
Device running too many processes. Restart and reduce load.

repair_steps: 1. Update iOS to latest version
2. Backup and restore iPhone via Finder/iTunes
3. Check the specific daemon — it indicates the failing subsystem
test_procedure: Note which daemon timed out
Cross-reference with other crash types in same time period

patterns.title: EXC_BAD_ACCESS — invalid memory address dereference
patterns.diagnosis: App tried to read/write a null or freed pointer. Classic use-after-free or dangling pointer bug.

probabilities.description: Object released while still referenced.
Pointer never assigned before dereference.
Buffer overflow overwrote return address.

repair_steps: Reproduce with Address Sanitizer enabled
Check recent Xcode updates for known bugs
Reinstall app — may clear corrupted state
test_procedure: Open app → trigger crash path
Update to latest iOS version
Reinstall app from App Store

patterns.title: EXC_BAD_ACCESS — memory protection violation
patterns.diagnosis: App attempted to write to read-only memory or execute non-executable memory. Often a code signing or JIT issue.

probabilities.description: App modified a constant or code segment.
Unsigned executable pages blocked by kernel.
JavaScript engine failed to map RWX pages.

repair_steps: Reinstall app
Check for iOS version compatibility
Report to app developer
test_procedure: Reinstall app → reproduce
Try older iOS if jailbroken env

patterns.title: SIGILL — illegal instruction / code signing failure
patterns.diagnosis: CPU encountered an invalid instruction. Often bad code signing, corrupted binary, or attempted execution of non-arm64 code.

probabilities.description: Download corrupted — reinstall fixes.
Provisioning profile mismatch or revoked.
x86 code attempted on arm64.

repair_steps: Delete and reinstall app
Clear iOS cache (Settings > General > iPhone Storage)
Restore iOS if persistent
test_procedure: Reinstall app
Check App Store for update

patterns.title: EXC_ARITHMETIC — division by zero or FP exception
patterns.diagnosis: Arithmetic exception: division by zero, integer overflow with trap, or floating-point exception.

probabilities.description: Denominator is zero in integer or FP division.
Swift/C integer overflow with trapping arithmetic.
NaN or infinity produced in FP operation.

repair_steps: Update app to latest version
Report crash to developer
test_procedure: Trigger specific app flow that leads to crash

patterns.title: EXC_BREAKPOINT — Swift runtime trap
patterns.diagnosis: Swift runtime detected a fatal condition: force-unwrap of nil, array out of bounds, integer overflow. Swift inserts trap instructions for these.

probabilities.description: app! on nil value → immediate trap.
Swift bounds-checking trap.

patterns.diagnosis: Objective-C exception accessing array/collection with invalid index. Common data race: count changes between check and access on different threads.

probabilities.description: objectAtIndex: called with index >= array.count, often from thread race.
numberOfRowsInSection disagrees with actual data — 'Invalid update' pattern.

probabilities.cause: String range operation on short string
probabilities.description: substringWithRange: with range beyond string length.

repair_steps: Update app to latest version
Report crash to developer
test_procedure: Reproduce specific action causing crash
Check for app update

patterns.title: Stack overflow — infinite recursion in app
patterns.diagnosis: App method calling itself infinitely until stack (~8MB main thread) is exhausted. Backtrace shows same 2-5 frames repeated hundreds of times.

probabilities.description: Method A calls B which calls A, or self-recursive without base case.
Recursive parser on deeply nested JSON/XML exceeding stack depth.
KVO observer triggering property change that fires same observer.

repair_steps: Update app to latest version
test_procedure: Backtrace shows same frames repeated hundreds of times
EXC_BAD_ACCESS at very low stack address

patterns.title: mic4 thermal sensor failure — rear microphone NTC
patterns.diagnosis: mic4 NTC thermal sensor on rear microphone flex is disconnected or damaged. Usually near rear camera module on iPhone 12+.

probabilities.description: mic4 on rear flex near camera module — flex replacement required.
mic4 connector not reseated or flex nicked during camera repair.
Trace from mic4 to thermal controller broken.

repair_steps: Replace rear flex cable carrying mic4 sensor
Reseat rear camera assembly connectors
Test rear microphone in Voice Memos after repair
test_procedure: Panic string: 'Missing sensor(s): mic4'
Video recording with phone facing away — check audio clarity

patterns.title: TG0H thermal sensor missing — housing temperature sensor
patterns.diagnosis: TG0H is the housing/enclosure temperature sensor, typically on battery assembly or dock flex. Its absence causes thermalmonitord to panic.

probabilities.cause: Battery connector not seated (TG0H on battery)
probabilities.description: TG0H often on battery assembly — reseat battery connector.

probabilities.cause: Dock flex cable TG0H sensor damage
probabilities.description: Dock flex carries TG0H if not on battery assembly.
Physical NTC thermistor on battery failed.

repair_steps: Reseat battery connector
Check dock flex cable if battery reseat doesn't resolve
Replace battery if NTC thermistor failed
test_procedure: Panic string: 'Missing sensor(s): TG0H'
Check battery health and connector seating

patterns.title: CPU wakeup rate panic — interrupt storm
patterns.diagnosis: CPU being woken up too frequently. Either a failing hardware component generating spurious interrupts, or a software timer running at excessive frequency.

probabilities.description: Failing touch IC, WiFi chip, or sensor generating rapid spurious interrupts.
App or daemon using high-frequency timers causing excessive wakes.
DMA controller completing transfers too rapidly causing interrupt flood.

repair_steps: DFU restore to rule out software cause
If hardware: identify interrupt source and replace component
Check for board-level short on interrupt lines
test_procedure: Panic string: 'Too many wakeups' or high wakeup count
DFU restore differentiates software vs hardware

patterns.title: GPS chip / GNSS failure
patterns.diagnosis: GPS/GNSS receiver failure. Antenna cable is often disconnected during repair. If antenna is connected, the GPS receiver IC itself has failed.

probabilities.description: GPS antenna coaxial cable disconnected during repair (back glass, battery).
GNSS IC failure — often shares silicon with WiFi/BT.
Baseband firmware issue affecting GPS — DFU restore may fix.

repair_steps: Check GPS antenna coaxial connector (thin cable, near top of device)
Test Maps in open sky — does dot appear and track?
DFU restore if GPS partially works
Replace GNSS IC if antenna connected and still no GPS
test_procedure: Open sky Maps test: GPS lock within 30s?
Accuracy should be < 5m
Compass app heading accuracy

patterns.title: OTA update failure — software update process crash
patterns.diagnosis: Over-the-air update failed. Most commonly insufficient storage for OTA preparation, network interruption, or NAND degradation preventing writes.

probabilities.description: OTA requires 1.5-3x update size in free space. Download succeeds but preparation fails.
Partial download or checksum mismatch.
Degraded NAND unable to write update. Manifests as repeated OTA failures.
Device-specific update entitlement check failed.

repair_steps: Free up storage: target 4GB+ free before attempting
Use iTunes/Finder for full restore (more reliable on degraded storage)
Update over WiFi with device plugged in
If OTA always fails: attempt DFU restore
test_procedure: Check available storage before update
If iTunes restore also fails (Error 4013/4014): NAND hardware failure

patterns.title: Tristar2 USB controller panic — iPhone 7/7+ USB failure
patterns.diagnosis: Tristar2 (updated Tristar USB controller IC on iPhone 7/8) BGA solder failure or corrosion. Causes USB communication failure, charge issues, and kernel panics.

probabilities.description: BGA solder joints cracked from thermal cycling or drop damage.
Corrosion on Tristar2 IC pads from liquid exposure.
Trace from Tristar2 to SoC or lightning port broken.

repair_steps: Reball Tristar2 IC (remove, clean pads, add fresh solder balls, resolder)
If pads damaged: Tristar2 replacement with jumper wires
Inspect lightning port for corrosion
test_procedure: Plug into Mac — does Finder/iTunes recognize device?
Check charging with multiple cables

patterns.title: Watchdog: app extension timeout
patterns.diagnosis: Share, Today widget, or notification extension took too long to initialize. Extension watchdog timeout is much shorter than main app (~5s for extensions vs. 20s for main app).

probabilities.cause: Extension blocking on main thread during init
probabilities.description: Heavy work in extension viewDidLoad — must be < 5s.
Extensions have stricter memory limits (60-120MB) than main app.

probabilities.cause: XPC communication timeout with host app
probabilities.description: Extension↔host app communication blocked.

repair_steps: Update app to latest version
Report to developer
test_procedure: Open Share Sheet or Notification Extension — does it load within 5s?
Check for app update

patterns.title: SMC I2C PMU error — power management bus failure
patterns.diagnosis: SMC I2C bus (i2Cm0/m1) error: communication failure between SoC and PMU IC. On A12+ models, i2Cm1 carries USB-PD IC communication.

probabilities.description: SMC i2Cm0 = USB IC and PMU communication bus. Failure indicates main PMU or USB controller IC.
Liquid damage corroding SMC I2C communication lines between SoC and PMU.
On iPhone XS/XR (A12): i2Cm1 carries USB Power Delivery IC. USBC IC or Tristar communication failed.

repair_steps: Check PMU communication lines for corrosion
PMU component replacement via microsoldering
For A12+ models: USB-PD IC may need replacement
test_procedure: Test charging, power on/off, battery percentage accuracy
SMC i2Cm0/m1 error in panic log

patterns.title: App killed by watchdog — launch/resume timeout (0x8badf00d)
patterns.diagnosis: SpringBoard's watchdog killed the app because it failed to respond within the allowed time (20s launch, 10s resume, 10s quit). The hex 0x8badf00d means 'ate bad food'.

probabilities.cause: Synchronous networking or I/O on main thread
probabilities.description: URLSession on main thread blocks UI thread past watchdog limit.
Schema migration runs synchronously at startup.
Class +initialize or viewDidLoad deadlocks.

repair_steps: Force quit app and relaunch
Update app to latest version
If recurring: offload launch work to background queues
test_procedure: Check Termination Description field for 'SpringBoard' and time elapsed
Confirm 0x8badf00d hex code in crash report

patterns.title: Swift fatal error — nil unwrap or precondition failure
patterns.diagnosis: Swift runtime raised a fatal error, most commonly from force-unwrapping a nil Optional (!) or a precondition()/fatalError() call.

probabilities.description: Value expected to be non-nil was nil at runtime.
Index exceeds collection bounds; Swift traps.
Developer marked a code path as unreachable, but it was reached.

repair_steps: Update app to latest version
Developer: replace ! with guard let / if let
Report crash log to developer
test_procedure: Identify the thread with Fatal error: in backtrace
Note the Swift source file referenced in crash log

patterns.title: ObjC exception / abort() — unhandled NSException
patterns.diagnosis: An unhandled Objective-C exception (NSException) caused abort() to be called, typically due to an invalid selector, out-of-bounds NSArray access, or custom exception.

probabilities.description: Method not found on target object; ObjC runtime throws.
Index beyond array count raises NSRangeException.
Third-party framework throws custom NSException.

repair_steps: Update app to latest version
Check crash log Last Exception Backtrace section
Developer: wrap risky calls in @try/@catch or use Swift-native APIs
test_procedure: Look for 'Last Exception Backtrace' section in crash log
Identify triggering method from ObjC frame

patterns.title: Memory protection violation — write to read-only region
patterns.diagnosis: The app attempted to write to a read-only memory region (e.g., the __TEXT segment or a mmap'd read-only file), triggering KERN_PROTECTION_FAILURE.

probabilities.description: JIT or dynamic code patching attempt blocked by W^X policy.
Memory-mapped file opened without PROT_WRITE.

probabilities.cause: Stack buffer overflow into guard page
probabilities.description: Local array overflow hits the stack guard page.

repair_steps: Update app — likely requires code fix from developer
Verify app uses standard iOS memory APIs
Report crash log to developer
test_procedure: Check faulting address vs process memory map in crash log
Identify if address falls in __TEXT segment

patterns.title: Restore error 4013 — USB communication failure during restore
patterns.diagnosis: iTunes/Finder restore error 4013 indicates the device lost USB connection during firmware restoration, preventing successful restore. Common with hardware-level connectivity issues.

probabilities.description: Damaged charging port causing intermittent USB data connection.
Faulty USB cable or hub causing dropped connection during restore.
USB multiplexer IC failing during high-bandwidth restore transfer.

repair_steps: Try different Apple Lightning/USB-C to USB-A cable
Connect directly to Mac USB-A port (no hub)
Try different Mac/PC
If persists: port or Tristar IC replacement
test_procedure: Use Apple-original cable directly in Mac USB-A port
Check if iPhone recognised in Finder before starting restore

patterns.title: Restore error 4014 — baseband not responding during restore
patterns.diagnosis: Error 4014 indicates the baseband modem did not respond during firmware restore. The baseband firmware could not be flashed, leaving the device in an incomplete restore state.

probabilities.description: Baseband IC physically damaged or failed, cannot be programmed.
Baseband NVM/NAND cannot accept new firmware writes.

probabilities.cause: Baseband power delivery issue during restore
probabilities.description: Unstable power during restore prevents baseband flash completion.

repair_steps: Retry DFU restore multiple times
Try restore on different Mac/PC
If persists: baseband IC hardware replacement required
test_procedure: Verify modem firmware version in Settings > General > About after restore
If version shows blank: baseband failed to flash

patterns.title: Restore error 4015 — Apple server communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: Error 4015 occurs when iTunes/Finder cannot communicate with Apple's activation/restore servers during the restore process, often a network or firewall issue.

probabilities.cause: Firewall or security software blocking Apple servers
probabilities.description: Corporate firewall or security software blocking access to apple.com restore endpoints.
Apple restore servers temporarily unavailable — check status.apple.com.
DNS or proxy not allowing connection to gs.apple.com.

repair_steps: Check status.apple.com for server issues
Disable VPN, firewall, or antivirus during restore
Try a different network (mobile hotspot)
Add gs.apple.com to firewall allowlist
test_procedure: Visit apple.com in browser on restore Mac to verify connectivity
Check status.apple.com for iOS restore service status

patterns.title: Restore error 9 — device timeout during DFU restore
patterns.diagnosis: Error 9 indicates the device timed out or the USB connection was interrupted during the critical DFU firmware flash phase, failing the restore.

probabilities.description: Device lost USB connection during firmware transfer.

probabilities.cause: Logic board hardware fault causing reset during flash
probabilities.description: Device hardware fault causing it to reset mid-restore.
Antivirus or MDM software interrupted USB communication.

repair_steps: Use Apple-original cable, connect directly to Mac USB-A port
Disable antivirus during restore
Try DFU mode instead of normal recovery mode
If hardware fault: board-level diagnosis
test_procedure: Enter DFU mode (not recovery mode) for restore attempt
Verify cable firmly connected throughout entire restore

patterns.title: Restore error 14 — IPSW download or verification failure
patterns.diagnosis: Error 14 indicates the iOS firmware file (IPSW) failed verification, either because the download was incomplete, the IPSW is corrupt, or the IPSW is not signed by Apple for this device.

probabilities.description: IPSW download was interrupted, checksum fails verification.

probabilities.cause: IPSW not compatible with this specific device model
probabilities.description: Wrong IPSW variant used for this device's regional model.

probabilities.cause: Apple not signing this iOS version anymore
probabilities.description: IPSW version is no longer signed (Apple stopped accepting it).

repair_steps: Delete the IPSW file and re-download via iTunes/Finder
Verify IPSW SHA-1 hash against IPSW.me listing
Use Finder/iTunes automatic IPSW selection rather than manual
test_procedure: Check IPSW file size matches expected — partial downloads are smaller
Verify signing status at ipsw.me or SHSH.host

patterns.title: Restore error 21 — custom IPSW / SHSH signing mismatch
patterns.diagnosis: Error 21 occurs when the IPSW's signing blobs (SHSH) do not match Apple's current signing server, or a modified/custom IPSW was used. Also seen when restoring to an unsigned iOS version.

probabilities.cause: Attempting to restore to unsigned iOS version
probabilities.description: Apple no longer signs the target iOS version, restore rejected.
Modified IPSW signature does not pass Apple's verification.
Jailbreak tool modified /etc/hosts; restoring Mac's hosts file fixes this.

repair_steps: Check /etc/hosts on Mac for gs.apple.com entry (jailbreak tools modify it)
Restore hosts file to default, retry restore
Restore to current signed iOS only via iTunes/Finder
test_procedure: Open Terminal, run: cat /etc/hosts | grep apple
Remove any 127.0.0.1 gs.apple.com line if present

patterns.title: Restore error 4005 — device busy or MDM restriction
patterns.diagnosis: Error 4005 typically means iTunes/Finder could not restore because the device is locked by MDM supervision, the USB port is in use, or a driver conflict exists.

probabilities.description: Corporate MDM policy prevents user from restoring supervised device.

probabilities.cause: USB port conflict or driver issue on Windows
probabilities.description: Apple Mobile Device USB driver not loaded or conflicting.

probabilities.cause: Device in active call or media playback during restore attempt
probabilities.description: Device not in idle state when restore initiated.

repair_steps: Check with IT admin if device is MDM-supervised
On Windows: uninstall/reinstall Apple Mobile Device Support
Put device in DFU mode before restore to bypass software locks
Try restore on Mac instead of Windows
test_procedure: Check Settings > General > Device Management for MDM profile
Try DFU mode restore on a Mac

patterns.title: SEP firmware update failure during OTA/restore
patterns.diagnosis: The Secure Enclave Processor firmware failed to update during an iOS OTA update or restore, leaving the device in a state where cryptographic operations may be unavailable.

probabilities.cause: SEP firmware flash error (NAND write fault)
probabilities.description: NAND error prevented new SEP firmware from being written.
Battery died or device powered off during SEP firmware write.

probabilities.cause: SEP hardware fault blocking firmware acceptance
probabilities.description: SEP hardware fault preventing firmware acceptance.

repair_steps: DFU restore — forces complete SEP firmware reflash
Ensure >50% battery before any OTA update
If DFU restore fails: Apple service required
test_procedure: After restore, test Face ID / Touch ID enrollment
Test Apple Pay — requires SEP to function

patterns.title: CPU thermal throttling event (CLPC)
patterns.diagnosis: The Closed-Loop Performance Controller (CLPC) reduced CPU/GPU frequency due to thermal limits being approached. This is normal protective behaviour but can cause app slowdowns.

probabilities.description: Gaming, video encoding, or ML inference running sustained at full load.
Thick or insulating case trapping heat; remove case to cool faster.

probabilities.cause: Degraded battery causing higher SoC resistance/heat
probabilities.description: Old battery with high internal resistance generates extra heat.

repair_steps: Remove case during extended heavy workloads
Update iOS — thermal management improvements common in updates
Replace battery if Battery Health below 80%
test_procedure: Check Settings > Battery > Battery Health
Note if throttling correlates with specific app or ambient temperature

patterns.title: Thermal sensor missing from system sensor report
patterns.diagnosis: One or more thermal sensors (NTC thermistors on PCB, battery, or flex cables) are absent from the sensor enumeration, preventing accurate thermal monitoring.

probabilities.cause: Flex cable with embedded NTC sensor disconnected
probabilities.description: Charging flex, display flex, or battery flex carrying NTC sensor is disconnected.
Micro-soldering repair lifted NTC sensor pad or broke trace.
Replacement battery missing the NTC thermistor or with damaged flex.

repair_steps: Identify which sensor is missing from log (mic1/mic2/baro/als)
Reconnect relevant flex cable
Replace flex cable with embedded sensor
test_procedure: Check Analytics logs for 'Missing sensor' entries
Cross-reference sensor ID with component location chart

patterns.title: Storage full — ENOSPC causing app crashes
patterns.diagnosis: The iPhone storage is full or critically low (< 1 GB free). Apps requiring temporary file space fail with ENOSPC errors, causing crashes or data loss.

probabilities.cause: Storage genuinely full from user data
probabilities.description: Photos, apps, or downloads have filled available NAND.
A malfunctioning app wrote large amounts of log/cache data.
Downloaded OTA update consuming reserved space.

repair_steps: Settings > General > iPhone Storage — review and delete large items
Delete unused apps
Offload photos to iCloud or Mac
Clear app caches via Settings > [App] > Clear Cache
test_procedure: Settings > General > iPhone Storage — check available space
If < 1 GB free: this is likely contributing to crashes

patterns.title: SQLite / CoreData database corruption
patterns.diagnosis: An app's SQLite database (often managed via CoreData) is corrupted, causing the app to crash on launch or when accessing the database.

probabilities.cause: Interrupted write (power loss / crash) left DB inconsistent
probabilities.description: Previous app crash during DB write left SQLite in corrupt state.

probabilities.cause: Multiple processes writing to same DB without WAL mode
probabilities.description: Concurrent writes without proper SQLite locking caused corruption.

probabilities.cause: NAND storage error during DB write
probabilities.description: Flash storage error corrupted DB page.

repair_steps: Delete and reinstall the app (fresh database)
Restore from iCloud/iTunes backup
Developer: enable SQLite WAL mode and use CoreData NSPersistentContainer
test_procedure: Delete app and reinstall — check if crash resolves
Check if crash happens immediately on launch (database accessed at startup)

patterns.title: OLED display burn-in / pixel retention
patterns.diagnosis: The OLED display shows permanent image retention (burn-in), where ghost images of previously displayed content are visible as permanent faint overlays.

probabilities.cause: Prolonged display of static content at high brightness
probabilities.description: Navigation bar, keyboard, or static UI elements burned in over time.
Using at 100% brightness accelerates OLED organic compound degradation.
Some panels more susceptible to retention; quality variance.

repair_steps: Enable 'Auto-Lock' to turn off display after 30s
Reduce display brightness below 80%
Enable 'Reduce White Point' in Accessibility
Display replacement if burn-in is severe and unacceptable
test_procedure: Display a solid grey or white screen — burn-in visible as ghost image
Use Apple's OLED screen burn-in test (grey background)

patterns.title: Green vertical line — OLED DDIC / display hardware fault
patterns.diagnosis: A permanent green vertical line on the display indicates a faulty OLED display driver IC (DDIC) or a damaged row/column driver within the OLED panel itself.

probabilities.cause: Display panel DDIC chip fault (often after drop)
probabilities.description: Impact to OLED stack damaged display driver IC.

probabilities.cause: Flex cable between DDIC and panel damaged
probabilities.description: Internal flex cable carries column/row drive signals; damage causes line.
Some iPhone X models exhibited green line from DDIC defect — Apple extended warranty.

repair_steps: Screen replacement (OLED panel with DDIC) required
Check if device is under Apple extended warranty program for this issue
Apple service for iPhone X green line (extended warranty program)
test_procedure: Display white or grey background — vertical green line will be clearly visible
Line present at all brightness levels = hardware fault

patterns.title: Wi-Fi driver / IO80211 kernel panic
patterns.diagnosis: The Wi-Fi kernel extension (IO80211Family) encountered a fatal assertion or null pointer, causing a kernel panic. Often related to specific network configurations or driver bugs.

probabilities.cause: Wi-Fi driver bug triggered by specific AP beacon
probabilities.description: Malformed or unusual Wi-Fi beacon/frame triggers driver assertion.

probabilities.cause: Wi-Fi chip firmware crash escalated to kernel
probabilities.description: WLAN firmware panic causes IO80211 kernel driver to panic.
BT/Wi-Fi coex shared resources cause concurrent access deadlock.

repair_steps: Update iOS (Wi-Fi driver fixes common in updates)
Forget Wi-Fi network and rejoin
Settings > General > Transfer or Reset > Reset Network Settings
test_procedure: Test on different Wi-Fi networks to isolate AP-specific trigger
Toggle Wi-Fi off/on after reset to test stability

patterns.title: Audio codec IC failure / driver panic
patterns.diagnosis: The audio codec IC (Cirrus Logic CS42L83 / 338S00509 etc.) failed to respond or triggered a driver panic, causing audio to stop working entirely.

probabilities.cause: Audio codec IC hardware fault (liquid damage common)
probabilities.description: Codec IC corroded or damaged — audio in/out all fail simultaneously.
Communication bus to codec chip damaged.
Codec firmware deadlocked; driver panic ensures clean recovery.

repair_steps: Test microphone (Voice Memos), speaker (Music), headphone (wired)
If all audio paths fail: codec IC replacement
Check for liquid damage on logic board around codec IC area
test_procedure: Record voice memo — test microphone input
Play music through speaker — test speaker output
Test with wired headphones — test headphone jack path

patterns.title: Sleep/Wake transition timeout — device stuck in power state
patterns.diagnosis: The device failed to complete a sleep or wake transition within the timeout window, causing either a hang or a panic to recover from the stuck power state.

probabilities.cause: Process holding power assertion preventing sleep
probabilities.description: App holding UIApplication backgroundTaskIdentifier blocking sleep.

probabilities.cause: Driver failing to acknowledge sleep request
probabilities.description: Peripheral driver not responding to sleep power state transition.
Overheat condition preventing device from entering low-power sleep state.

repair_steps: Force restart iPhone (hold Side + Volume Down)
Update iOS and all apps
Identify power-assertion-holding app via Analytics logs
test_procedure: Check if sleep button reliably locks device
Verify screen turns off after Auto-Lock period

patterns.title: Restore error 4002 — firmware personalisation / activation failure
patterns.diagnosis: Error 4002 indicates Apple's personalisation server (Tatsu/TSS) could not personalise the firmware IPSW for this specific device ECID, blocking restore.

probabilities.description: Apple's firmware signing/personalisation server temporarily unavailable.
Firewall or proxy blocking connections to gs.apple.com TSS endpoint.

probabilities.cause: iOS version no longer signed by Apple
probabilities.description: Target iOS version no longer accepted by TSS; only latest iOS signed.

repair_steps: Check status.apple.com for TSS service status
Disable VPN/proxy, try restore again
Let iTunes/Finder choose iOS version automatically
Try from different network
test_procedure: Check status.apple.com — iOS Software Restore section
Verify gs.apple.com accessible: ping gs.apple.com from Terminal

patterns.title: Front (FaceTime) camera hardware failure
patterns.diagnosis: The front-facing FaceTime camera module is not responding or producing invalid image data. Affects video calls, selfies, and Face ID on non-TrueDepth models.

probabilities.description: Flex cable connecting front camera to logic board broken or pinched.
CMOS sensor or lens assembly in front camera failed.

probabilities.cause: ISP crash for front camera pipeline
probabilities.description: Image Signal Processor crashed during front camera initialisation.

repair_steps: Test in Camera app — tap flip icon to switch to front camera
Check for 'camera unavailable' error in Camera app
Front camera module or screen assembly replacement
test_procedure: Open Camera app, switch to front-facing — should show preview
Test FaceTime video call — front camera required

patterns.title: Rear camera hardware failure (main / ultra-wide / telephoto)
patterns.diagnosis: One or more rear camera modules (main, ultra-wide, or telephoto) failed or are producing errors. The Camera app may show 'Camera Unavailable' or a black preview.

probabilities.cause: Rear camera flex or connector damage
probabilities.description: Camera module flex cable or ZIF connector broken from impact.
Optical Image Stabilisation magnet/coil assembly damaged causing vibration/failure.
Image sensor physically damaged — commonly from impact to rear glass.

repair_steps: Test each camera: main, ultra-wide, and telephoto in Camera app
Check for physical impact damage to rear camera housing
Rear camera module replacement (main or telephoto/ultra-wide assembly)
test_procedure: Open Camera app — try 0.5x (ultra-wide), 1x (main), 2x/3x (telephoto)
Check if OIS stabilisation works — should smooth out hand shake in preview

patterns.title: Unaligned memory access (BUS_ADRALN)
patterns.diagnosis: A kernel or app-level access occurred at a misaligned address. ARM strictly requires aligned loads/stores for certain instruction types; misaligned access triggers a SIGBUS.

probabilities.cause: C struct packed without proper alignment
probabilities.description: __attribute__((packed)) struct member accessed directly.
Driver sets up DMA to a non-aligned memory region.
Deserialised binary data at odd address cast to pointer.

repair_steps: Update iOS and affected app
Developer: use memcpy() for unaligned reads
Report crash to developer with full log
test_procedure: Check faulting address for alignment (odd address for 16/32-bit type)
Note SIGBUS code BUS_ADRALN in crash header

patterns.title: Synchronous External Abort (hardware bus fault)
patterns.diagnosis: An ARM Synchronous External Abort (SEA) was raised, indicating a hardware-level error on the system bus — often memory controller, fabric interconnect, or cache error.

probabilities.description: Memory subsystem returned an uncorrectable error.
Driver accessed a memory-mapped register while peripheral was powered off.
Transient error on Apple internal high-speed bus fabric.

repair_steps: Update iOS
If hardware error: check for physical damage
Apple service / component-level repair if recurring
test_procedure: Look for 'synchronous external abort' or 'SEA' in panic text
Check if panic correlates with specific app or thermal condition

patterns.title: Kernel VM fault — virtual memory mapping failure
patterns.diagnosis: The kernel's virtual memory fault handler encountered an unresolvable page fault — the page could not be faulted in from the backing store or swap.

probabilities.description: Page backed by flash storage cannot be read due to NAND error.
Internal vm_map data structure corrupted, leading to un-resolvable fault.
No physical memory available to satisfy page-in request.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Free storage space (< 10% free triggers aggressive compression)
DFU restore if recurring
test_procedure: Confirm 'vm_fault' in kernel panic backtrace
Check available storage space

patterns.title: Physical memory bus error (BUS_ADRERR)
patterns.diagnosis: A physical bus error occurred when accessing a real hardware address — distinct from alignment (BUS_ADRALN), this indicates a hardware-level memory access fault.

probabilities.description: DRAM cell failure causing uncorrectable read errors.
Driver reads memory-mapped register of unpowered peripheral.
Package-on-package LPDDR solder issue on logic board.

repair_steps: Run Apple Diagnostics via support.apple.com
Apple service center — board-level RAM replacement
Data backup immediately — hardware failure is progressive
test_procedure: Confirm BUS_ADRERR code in crash report exception header
Check if crash correlates with thermal throttling or heavy RAM usage

patterns.title: Floating-point / SIMD trap in kernel (MSR/FPSIMD)
patterns.diagnosis: An FP/SIMD instruction was executed in a context where it is not permitted (e.g., interrupt handler), or an illegal system register access caused a CPU exception.

probabilities.description: NEON/SIMD instruction used in IRQ handler without saving SIMD state.
Driver reads/writes an EL2/EL3 register from EL1.

probabilities.cause: Software update resolving CPU state bug
probabilities.description: Known microarchitecture errata.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Report panic log to Apple Feedback Assistant
DFU restore if recurring
test_procedure: Look for 'FPSIMD', 'MSR', or 'CPACR' in panic string
Note which process triggered the fault

patterns.title: APFS filesystem panic — volume corruption detected
patterns.diagnosis: The APFS filesystem driver detected internal inconsistency or corruption and triggered a kernel panic to prevent further data corruption.

probabilities.cause: NAND write failure causing APFS inconsistency
probabilities.description: Partial write left APFS B-tree in inconsistent state.

probabilities.cause: Unexpected power loss during write transaction
probabilities.description: Battery death mid-write left APFS journal in bad state.

probabilities.cause: APFS software bug in iOS version
probabilities.description: Edge-case APFS path triggers assertion, fixed in later iOS.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Connect to Mac and run 'idevicediagnostics' — NAND check
DFU restore to repartition and reformat APFS volume
test_procedure: Search for 'APFS' and 'Corruption' or 'assertion' in panic string
Check storage capacity — very full storage increases APFS stress

patterns.title: IOKit assertion failure — driver locking violation
patterns.diagnosis: An IOKit driver assertion failed, often due to a locking violation — accessing an IOService without the required gate/lock held, or a driver lifecycle assertion.

probabilities.cause: IOService method called from wrong context
probabilities.description: Driver method invoked without workloop lock; assertion fires.
Client attached to service being terminated simultaneously.
Accessory-related kext violates IOKit contract.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Disconnect external accessories and retest
DFU restore if panic continues
test_procedure: Look for 'IOKit' and 'assert' or 'locking' in panic string
Note IOService subclass name in backtrace

patterns.title: CPU watchdog timeout — kernel deadlock detected
patterns.diagnosis: The hardware CPU watchdog timer expired because a CPU core was stuck for too long without servicing the watchdog, indicating a kernel deadlock or severe livelock.

probabilities.description: Two kernel threads each hold a lock the other needs.
High-frequency interrupts prevent watchdog pet.
Extreme thermal throttling or power instability causes CPU stall.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Check for apps causing extreme CPU load
Apple service center if panic correlates with temperature
test_procedure: Search for 'CPUWatchdog' or 'watchdog timer expired' in panic
Check if device was under heavy load or thermal stress

patterns.title: DART / IOMMU DMA translation fault
patterns.diagnosis: Apple's DART (Device Address Resolution Table / IOMMU) blocked an illegal DMA transaction from a peripheral, triggering a kernel panic.

probabilities.cause: Peripheral DMA to unmapped physical address
probabilities.description: Hardware component attempted DMA to address not in its DART table.
DART mapping table corrupted leading to false fault.

probabilities.cause: Driver fails to map DMA buffer before use
probabilities.description: Driver starts DMA transfer before calling IODMACommand::prepare().

repair_steps: Update iOS
Check for accessory causing fault (note peripheral in panic)
Apple service if hardware DMA component fails
test_procedure: Search for 'DART' or 'dart_fault' in panic string
Note which peripheral/driver is named at top of backtrace

patterns.title: Jetsam idle process kill (normal lifecycle)
patterns.diagnosis: Jetsam killed a process marked as idle-exit because memory pressure required freeing resources. This is normal iOS behaviour for apps that registered for idle-exit.

probabilities.description: iOS killed idle background process to free RAM — expected behaviour.
Foreground app spike caused cascade of idle-exit kills.
Background daemon incorrectly registered as idle-exit eligible.

repair_steps: No action required — this is normal iOS memory management
If app loses state unexpectedly: developer should save state before idle-exit
test_procedure: Confirm 'reason: idle' in JetsamEvent
Verify this is not occurring excessively for critical daemons

patterns.title: Jetsam physical memory footprint limit exceeded
patterns.diagnosis: The process exceeded the physical memory footprint limit (phys_footprint) tracked by the kernel, triggering a jetsam memory-limit kill.

probabilities.cause: App's physical footprint grew beyond entitlement
probabilities.description: Physical footprint (dirty + compressed pages) exceeded allowed limit.

probabilities.cause: IOKit metal/GPU buffers counted in footprint
probabilities.description: GPU/Metal resource allocations counted toward process footprint.
Large mmap'd regions inflating reported footprint.

repair_steps: Update app
Developer: use Metal resource options to reduce GPU memory impact
Developer: call trim() on SQLite DBs to reduce dirty pages
test_procedure: Check JetsamEvent for 'reason: memory-limit' and phys_footprint value
Compare footprint against per-process limit in log

patterns.title: VM compressor space exhausted — swap-like memory full
patterns.diagnosis: The iOS VM compressor (which replaces swap on flash-constrained devices) ran out of capacity. Jetsam must aggressively kill processes to reduce memory pressure.

probabilities.description: Too many suspended apps compressed the compressor to capacity.

probabilities.cause: Single app with massive memory balloon
probabilities.description: Game or media app expanded memory far beyond what compressor can handle.

probabilities.cause: Low storage space limiting compressor backing
probabilities.description: Compressor's backing store on NAND is near capacity.

repair_steps: Restart iPhone to clear compressor state
Free at least 2 GB of storage
Close all background apps before launching memory-intensive apps
test_procedure: Search for 'compressor_space_shortage' in JetsamEvent log
Check available storage space (Settings > General > iPhone Storage)

patterns.title: Kernel zone map exhaustion
patterns.diagnosis: The kernel's zone allocator ran out of virtual address space in the zone map, causing the kernel to panic or aggressively kill processes to reclaim zone memory.

probabilities.cause: Kernel memory leak from driver or app
probabilities.description: A driver or system call allocates zone memory and never frees it.

probabilities.cause: Very large number of threads or sockets
probabilities.description: Thousands of threads/sockets exhaust thread or socket zone.
Mach IPC ports or messages accumulating without being consumed.

repair_steps: Restart iPhone
Update iOS
Identify misbehaving app via crash log process name
test_procedure: Search for 'zone_map_exhaustion' or 'zone_gc' in panic/jetsam log
Note which zone was exhausted (zone name in log)

patterns.title: JetsamEvent diagnostic log — memory pressure episode
patterns.diagnosis: A JetsamEvent log was recorded, documenting a memory pressure episode where one or more processes were killed. The log contains per-process memory snapshots at time of event.

probabilities.description: JetsamEvent logs are common and often benign.
Heavy app pushed system into jetsam territory.
Background process memory growing until jetsam triggered.

repair_steps: Review largestProcess field to identify memory consumer
Update apps consuming most memory
Restart iPhone if events are frequent
test_procedure: Open JetsamEvent in Settings > Privacy > Analytics
Note largestProcess and its rpages value

patterns.title: App killed for excessive CPU wakeups (EXC_RESOURCE WAKEUPS)
patterns.diagnosis: The kernel terminated the app because it was waking the CPU too frequently (typically > 150 wakeups/second sustained for 300 seconds), draining battery excessively.

probabilities.description: Background task using Timer or dispatch_after polling at high frequency.
App sending network pings more frequently than allowed.
CFRunLoop source or NSRunLoop observer firing without yield.

repair_steps: Update app
Developer: use NSURLSession background sessions and push notifications
Developer: replace polling timers with event-driven callbacks
test_procedure: Confirm RESOURCE_TYPE_WAKEUPS and wakeup count in crash header
Check battery usage in Settings > Battery for excessive drain from this app

patterns.title: thermalmonitord watchdog timeout (thermal monitor hung)
patterns.diagnosis: The thermal monitor daemon (thermalmonitord) timed out. This is distinct from a thermal shutdown — the monitoring process itself hung, leaving thermal management blind.

probabilities.description: One or more thermal sensors stopped responding, hanging the monitor.
I2C/SPI thermal sensor bus timeout causes daemon to block indefinitely.
SMC unresponsive to thermal data requests from thermalmonitord.

repair_steps: Restart iPhone
Check for missing sensor messages in logs (mic1, mic2, baro, als)
Apple service if thermal sensors persistently fail
test_procedure: Check Analytics logs for 'Missing sensor' entries alongside thermalmonitord crash
Monitor device temperature during heavy use

patterns.title: asd (Apple System Diagnostics) daemon timeout
patterns.diagnosis: The Apple System Diagnostics daemon (asd) timed out during a diagnostic routine. This is often seen when diagnostics are running in the background during a repair workflow.

probabilities.cause: Hardware component not responding during diagnostic
probabilities.description: ASD test queried a hardware component that did not respond.

probabilities.cause: asd invoked at wrong time (insufficient power)
probabilities.description: Diagnostics launched with low battery — insufficient power for all tests.

probabilities.cause: asd software issue in iOS version
probabilities.description: Known diagnostic tool bug fixed in later iOS.

repair_steps: Do not use device during ASD diagnostic run
Ensure >50% battery before running diagnostics
Update iOS
test_procedure: Run Apple's official diagnostic via support.apple.com/diagnostics
Note which component caused the timeout

patterns.title: SpringBoard crash / watchdog termination (UI unresponsive)
patterns.diagnosis: SpringBoard (the iOS home screen process) was killed by its watchdog or crashed, causing the device to respring or reboot. Indicates serious UI framework or memory issue.

probabilities.description: SpringBoard itself ran out of memory due to widget/notification accumulation.
UIKit operation on main thread deadlocked, watchdog killed it.
Third-party SpringBoard plugin caused crash.

repair_steps: Restart iPhone
Remove recently installed apps (especially those with widgets)
Update iOS
If jailbroken: remove recently added tweaks
test_procedure: Check if respring happens at consistent Home Screen action
Note if it correlates with a specific widget or notification

patterns.title: AGX GPU firmware hang
patterns.diagnosis: The Apple AGX GPU firmware became unresponsive, causing the kernel to detect a GPU hang and recover or panic. Often seen with graphically intensive apps or Metal workloads.

probabilities.description: App's Metal compute or vertex shader ran indefinitely, hanging GPU.

probabilities.cause: GPU firmware bug in iOS version
probabilities.description: Known AGX firmware issue triggering hang under specific workload.
Extreme throttling slows GPU below firmware timeout threshold.

repair_steps: Update iOS (AGX firmware updates included)
Force quit the app causing the hang
If recurring in all apps: DFU restore
test_procedure: Note which app was running when panic occurred
Check if lower graphics settings in app prevent recurrence

patterns.title: IOSurface GPU memory allocation fault
patterns.diagnosis: IOSurface (the shared GPU/CPU memory buffer system) encountered an allocation failure or memory fault, impacting rendering pipeline and causing crash.

probabilities.cause: GPU memory exhausted — IOSurface alloc failed
probabilities.description: Too many large IOSurface buffers allocated simultaneously.

probabilities.cause: IOSurface port leaked — Mach port exhaustion
probabilities.description: IOSurface Mach ports leaked until task port limit reached.
App attempted to access another process's IOSurface without permission.

repair_steps: Update app and iOS
Force quit memory-heavy apps
Restart iPhone to clear GPU memory state
test_procedure: Check crash log for 'IOSurface' in faulting thread
Test with fewer simultaneous apps using GPU

patterns.title: Metal shader compilation failure
patterns.diagnosis: A Metal shader failed to compile at runtime, causing the rendering pipeline to fail. Can be triggered by a corrupt Metal binary cache or incompatible shader code.

probabilities.description: Pre-compiled Metal shader cache corrupted; fallback compile fails.

probabilities.cause: Shader targeting wrong GPU feature set
probabilities.description: Shader uses MTLGPUFamily not supported by this device's AGX version.

probabilities.cause: Metal compiler bug in iOS version
probabilities.description: iOS Metal compiler regression causing valid shader to fail.

repair_steps: Delete and reinstall the app (clears Metal cache)
Update iOS and the app
Developer: validate shaders on all target GPU families
test_procedure: Delete app, reinstall, check if crash recurs
Note app and iOS version where issue occurs

patterns.title: IOMobileFramebuffer / display pipe panic
patterns.diagnosis: The display framebuffer driver (IOMobileFramebuffer) panicked, causing the display pipeline to hang. Results in a full kernel panic or forced reboot.

probabilities.cause: Display pipe underrun under extreme GPU load
probabilities.description: GPU could not push frames fast enough, display pipe underran.
IOMobileFramebuffer driver hit an assertion or timeout.
Display panel or DDIC chip fault causing framebuffer error.

repair_steps: Update iOS
If display shows artifacts before panic: display hardware inspection
DFU restore to rule out software cause
test_procedure: Note if visual glitches appear before panic
Check panic backtrace for IOMobileFramebuffer frames

patterns.title: HDCP content protection authentication failure
patterns.diagnosis: HDCP (High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection) authentication failed during external display or AirPlay session, preventing DRM-protected content from displaying.

probabilities.description: Connected display or adapter does not support HDCP 2.2 required by content.
Network latency caused HDCP handshake to time out.
iOS HDCP stack regression in specific version.

repair_steps: Use Apple-certified HDMI adapter
Ensure display supports HDCP 2.2
Update iOS and Apple TV / AirPlay receiver firmware
test_procedure: Test with Apple Lightning/USB-C Digital AV Adapter
Try non-DRM content to confirm adapter/display works otherwise

patterns.title: GPU command buffer timeout (GPUC supervisor fault)
patterns.diagnosis: The GPU supervisor (GPUC) detected a Metal command buffer that did not complete within the hardware timeout limit, triggering a GPU reset or kernel panic.

probabilities.cause: Metal compute shader infinite loop or infinite barrier
probabilities.description: Compute shader ran beyond the GPU hardware timeout.

probabilities.cause: Large render pass taking too long
probabilities.description: Enormous single render pass exceeded GPU execution time budget.

probabilities.cause: GPU power gating during command execution
probabilities.description: Aggressive power gating interrupted a long-running GPU command.

repair_steps: Update app (developer should use fences/smaller command buffers)
Update iOS
Avoid apps with extremely heavy GPU workloads on warm device
test_procedure: Check crash log for 'GPUC' or 'GPUSupervisor'
Test if crash is reproducible with consistent GPU workload

patterns.title: ICE (in-call engine) modem crash
patterns.diagnosis: Apple's ICE (In-Call Engine) modem software stack crashed. ICE handles the protocol stack between the iOS telephony layer and the baseband chip.

probabilities.cause: Abnormal network signalling triggering ICE assert
probabilities.description: Malformed or unusual network packet caused ICE stack assertion.

probabilities.cause: ICE software bug in iOS version
probabilities.description: Known ICE crash fixed in subsequent iOS release.

probabilities.cause: RF interference destabilising modem state machine
probabilities.description: Extreme RF environment caused unexpected modem state transitions.

repair_steps: Update iOS and carrier settings
Toggle Airplane mode on/off to reset modem stack
Contact carrier if specific network triggers crash
test_procedure: Check if crash occurs during calls or data sessions specifically
