Test on different carrier network if possible

patterns.title: PMB9943 (Infineon/Intel) baseband modem fault
patterns.diagnosis: The PMB9943 baseband modem IC (used in older LTE iPhones with Intel modem) encountered a fatal hardware fault or firmware crash.

probabilities.description: PMB9943 chip physical failure — solder joint, ESD damage, or aging.

probabilities.cause: Baseband firmware incompatibility with iOS version
probabilities.description: Firmware mismatch after partial update.

probabilities.cause: RF power amplifier fault affecting modem
probabilities.description: PA fault causing modem to detect over/under voltage.

repair_steps: Update iOS carrier settings
DFU restore to force complete baseband reflash
Apple service / baseband IC replacement
test_procedure: Test cellular in multiple coverage areas
Check Modem Firmware version in Settings > General > About

patterns.title: Qualcomm X55/X65 5G baseband fault
patterns.diagnosis: The Qualcomm X55 (iPhone 12 series) or X65 (iPhone 13-14 series) 5G modem encountered a firmware crash or hardware fault, causing cellular connectivity loss or device crash.

probabilities.description: 5G band switching caused modem state machine to enter undefined state.
Qualcomm modem thermal throttle causing firmware timeout.
SIM card interface error causing modem authentication failure.

repair_steps: Toggle Airplane mode, update carrier settings
Disable 5G (Settings > Cellular > Voice & Data > LTE) temporarily
Update iOS, DFU restore if persistent
test_procedure: Test with 5G disabled to isolate 5G band switching issue
Check if crash correlates with switching between 5G and LTE

patterns.title: SIM card communication failure
patterns.diagnosis: The SIM card interface (physical or eSIM) failed to communicate with the baseband modem, causing cellular service loss and potentially a baseband crash.

probabilities.cause: Physical SIM card damaged or misseated
probabilities.description: SIM card contacts corroded, bent, or SIM not fully seated in tray.
SIM tray or reader on logic board damaged.
eSIM profile data on eUICC corrupted.

repair_steps: Eject and reseat SIM card, inspect contacts
Try a new SIM card from carrier
For eSIM issues: Settings > Cellular > Remove eSIM, re-add
test_procedure: Check Settings > Cellular for 'No SIM' indicator
Test with a known-good SIM from same carrier

patterns.title: RF frontend / antenna fault causing no service
patterns.diagnosis: The RF front-end (antenna switch, PA module, or RFFE interface) has a hardware fault, resulting in persistent 'No Service' and potentially baseband crashes.

probabilities.description: Cellular antenna flex cable damaged from impact or bending.
RFFE PA module failed — common after liquid damage.
NVM RF calibration data corrupted requiring reflash.

repair_steps: Test in area with strong signal coverage
DFU restore to attempt RF calibration recovery
Board-level repair: RF PA module or antenna flex replacement
test_procedure: Check signal bar count and Field Test mode (*3001#12345#*)
Test outdoors with clear line of sight to cell tower

patterns.title: IMEI missing or corrupted — baseband NVM fault
patterns.diagnosis: The device's IMEI is missing or shows as zeros/invalid, indicating the baseband NVM (Non-Volatile Memory) storing calibration and identity data is corrupted or the baseband has been replaced.

probabilities.cause: Baseband NVM data erased or corrupted
probabilities.description: IMEI/calibration data in baseband NVM overwritten or failed.

probabilities.cause: Baseband chip replaced without NVM programming
probabilities.description: Component-level repair replaced baseband IC without restoring NVM.
Partial or interrupted DFU left NVM in unprogrammed state.

repair_steps: DFU restore via iTunes/Finder — may restore NVM backup
If IMEI still missing: baseband NVM re-programming required (specialist repair)
Apple service if unable to restore IMEI
test_procedure: Check Settings > General > About > IMEI
Dial *#06# — IMEI should appear on screen

patterns.title: Baseband-SEP authentication / certificate mismatch
patterns.diagnosis: The baseband firmware failed authentication against the Secure Enclave, indicating a version mismatch, corrupt firmware signature, or non-genuine baseband component.

probabilities.cause: Baseband firmware version incompatible with iOS SEP
probabilities.description: Partial update left baseband at version incompatible with current SEP.
Replacement baseband IC not matching device's SEP pairing.

probabilities.cause: Corrupt baseband firmware signature in NVM
probabilities.description: Code signing data in baseband NVM corrupted.

repair_steps: DFU restore to fully re-sign and flash baseband
If non-genuine baseband: original part required for authentication
Apple service — paired component replacement
test_procedure: Check if cellular is functional at all
DFU restore and verify modem firmware version restored correctly

patterns.title: Secure Boot verification failure
patterns.diagnosis: The device failed Secure Boot chain verification (img4 / LocalPolicy). The boot firmware, OS image, or SEP could not be verified cryptographically.

probabilities.cause: iOS firmware file corrupted in NAND
probabilities.description: System firmware files on NAND corrupted; img4 validation fails.
OTA update interrupted, leaving system partition in partial state.
Component replacement (Screen, Face ID) invalidated LocalPolicy.

repair_steps: DFU restore via iTunes/Finder on Mac/PC
Ensure device is paired before attempting restore (LocalPolicy)
Apple service if DFU restore repeatedly fails
test_procedure: Attempt DFU restore
After restore, verify Touch ID / Face ID re-enrollment works

patterns.title: Touch ID sensor failure / SEP communication fault
patterns.diagnosis: Touch ID fingerprint recognition failed permanently. Either the sensor hardware is damaged, or the SEP pairing between the home button and main logic board is broken.

probabilities.description: Touch ID sensor damaged by drop, water, or unauthorized repair.

probabilities.cause: SEP pairing invalidated after screen/board repair
probabilities.description: Home button is paired to its original logic board; transplant breaks Touch ID.
Connector or flex cable between home button and logic board damaged.

repair_steps: Test with original home button if available
Note: Touch ID component is paired — only Apple or official partner can restore
Disable Touch ID and use passcode only if hardware replacement not possible
test_procedure: Settings > Touch ID & Passcode — attempt fingerprint enrollment
Check if 'Touch ID is not available' error appears

patterns.title: AMFI code signature / trust cache violation
patterns.diagnosis: Apple Mobile File Integrity (AMFI) rejected a process due to invalid code signature, missing trust cache entry, or codesigning policy violation.

probabilities.cause: App binary corrupted or modified post-install
probabilities.description: App binary on storage modified; AMFI signature check fails at launch.
Enterprise-signed app's provisioning profile has expired.

probabilities.cause: Jailbreak / tweak injection blocked by AMFI
probabilities.description: Jailbreak tools attempt to inject code blocked by AMFI.

repair_steps: Delete and reinstall app from App Store
Renew enterprise provisioning profile if applicable
Restore device if jailbreak interference suspected
test_procedure: Delete app and reinstall — check if AMFI error resolved
Check device trust for enterprise apps: Settings > General > VPN & Device Management

patterns.title: AppleSEP IPC / mailbox communication timeout
patterns.diagnosis: The AP-side SEP manager (AppleSEPManager) timed out waiting for a response from the SEP processor via the mailbox IPC channel.

probabilities.cause: SEP processor hung during cryptographic operation
probabilities.description: SEP busy with key derivation; mailbox response timed out.

probabilities.cause: SEP firmware crash caused silence on mailbox
probabilities.description: SEP firmware crashed internally without sending error response.
SEP power domain unexpectedly gated off during active IPC.

repair_steps: Restart iPhone
Update iOS
If recurring: Apple service — SEP hardware fault
test_procedure: Test Face ID, Touch ID, and Apple Pay after restart
Check if keychain operations (password autofill) work

patterns.title: Face ID TrueDepth hardware component failure
patterns.diagnosis: One or more TrueDepth camera components (flood illuminator, dot projector, or IR camera) have failed, making Face ID permanently unavailable.

probabilities.description: Physical impact or unauthorized screen replacement damaged TrueDepth module.
Flex cable connector between TrueDepth and board loosened or damaged.
VCSEL array or structured light projector component failed from aging.

repair_steps: Test Face ID in Settings > Face ID & Passcode
Only Apple or authorized service provider can replace TrueDepth module (paired component)
Use passcode as alternative
test_procedure: Settings > Face ID & Passcode > Set Up Face ID — check for hardware error
Note 'Face ID is not available' message

patterns.title: SPMI bus communication fault (PMU subsystem)
patterns.diagnosis: The SPMI (System Power Management Interface) bus between the AP and PMU/PMIC experienced a transaction failure, disrupting power rail monitoring and control.

probabilities.description: PMIC chip partially failed causing SPMI bus errors.

probabilities.cause: SPMI bus solder joint / ESD damage
probabilities.description: PCB trace or solder joint on SPMI bus damaged.
PMU firmware edge case causing SPMI transaction hang.

repair_steps: Update iOS — PMU firmware updates included
Apple service / PMU IC replacement if hardware fault confirmed
Board-level micro-soldering for SPMI trace repair
test_procedure: Check for random shutdowns and charging issues alongside crash
Monitor battery percentage stability

patterns.title: Charging IC / BMIC hardware fault
patterns.diagnosis: The battery management IC (BMIC/charger IC) reported a fault condition: VBUS anomaly, ACOK failure, or charger IC hardware fault preventing safe charging.

probabilities.cause: Charging IC (BQ25 / SN2671 / Hammerhead) hardware fault
probabilities.description: Charger IC failed — phone may not charge or charges erratically.
Liquid in Lightning/USB-C port causing VBUS anomalies.
Non-compliant charger triggering ACOK fault in charging IC.

repair_steps: Clean Lightning/USB-C port with isopropyl alcohol
Test with Apple-certified cable and charger
If phone doesn't charge: charging IC replacement required
test_procedure: Test charging with Apple original cable and 20W adapter
Check if 'Charging Not Supported' alert appears with non-Apple accessories

patterns.title: Power Management Interrupt (PMI) missing fault
patterns.diagnosis: The Power Management Interrupt (PMI) signal from the PMIC to the AP is missing or not asserted, preventing the kernel from receiving power state change notifications.

probabilities.cause: PMU/PMIC hardware fault — interrupt line dead
probabilities.description: PMU chip failed or interrupt pin open-circuit.

probabilities.cause: PCB trace fault on PMI line
probabilities.description: Broken PCB trace carrying PMI interrupt signal.
iOS driver accidentally masking PMI interrupt.

repair_steps: DFU restore to rule out firmware cause
Apple service / board-level PMU repair
Micro-soldering on PMI trace if trace fault confirmed
test_procedure: Check if random shutdowns accompany this crash
Test battery percentage reading stability

patterns.title: SN2671 wireless charging / MagSafe IC fault
patterns.diagnosis: The SN2671 wireless charging controller (handles Qi and MagSafe wireless charging) reported a fault, preventing wireless charging or causing power instability during wireless charging.

probabilities.description: Wireless charging controller chip failed.
Charging coil delaminated or shorted.

probabilities.cause: MagSafe magnet array interfering with IC
probabilities.description: MagSafe alignment magnets demagnetized or foreign metal particles.

repair_steps: Test with Apple MagSafe charger only
Remove phone case and retry wireless charging
If wireless charging fails: SN2671 IC or coil replacement
test_procedure: Place on Apple MagSafe charger — check if charging indicator appears
Test Qi charging with standard Qi pad

patterns.title: Battery fuel gauge / SoC measurement fault
patterns.diagnosis: The battery fuel gauge (coulomb counter / gas gauge IC) is reporting inaccurate State-of-Charge or has failed, causing the OS to receive incorrect battery percentage data.

probabilities.cause: Aged battery causing gauge calibration drift
probabilities.description: Old battery's impedance has changed, causing gauge to miscalculate SoC.
New battery installed without recalibrating fuel gauge.
Battery management IC gauge circuit failure.

repair_steps: Calibrate battery: drain fully, charge to 100% uninterrupted
Replace battery through Apple service (recalibrates gauge)
Settings > Battery > Battery Health — check for 'Service' recommendation
test_procedure: Check for sudden jumps in battery percentage
Note if phone shuts down above 0% — classic gauge drift symptom

patterns.title: PMIC power rail / voltage regulator fault
patterns.diagnosis: A power regulator or LDO (Low-Dropout regulator) managed by the PMIC reported a fault — undervoltage, overcurrent, or dropout — causing system instability.

probabilities.cause: PMIC power rail fault after liquid damage
probabilities.description: Corrosion on PMIC rails causing regulator fault.

probabilities.cause: Component drawing excessive current on rail
probabilities.description: Shorted peripheral causing current limit on shared power rail.
PMIC regulator degraded after years of thermal cycling.

repair_steps: Inspect for liquid damage indicators (LCI stickers)
Apple service / board-level PMIC work
Identify shorted component via current injection
test_procedure: Check for liquid damage in SIM tray slot (LCI indicator)
Measure battery voltage under load with external meter if possible

patterns.title: Charger detection / Apple Charger disconnect event
patterns.diagnosis: The charger detection subsystem reported an unexpected disconnect or failure to identify the connected charger type, causing charging to stop or fail.

probabilities.description: Dirty connector causing intermittent charger detection.
Port flex cable pins bent or broken.
Damaged USB cable causing intermittent connection.

repair_steps: Clean port with soft brush and compressed air
Test with original Apple cable
Replace charging port flex if cleaning doesn't resolve
test_procedure: Check if charging indicator appears and stays stable when connected
Try multiple cables — rule out cable fault first

patterns.title: Accelerometer sensor failure (STMicro / LIS series)
patterns.diagnosis: The accelerometer (typically STMicroelectronics LIS2DH or similar) stopped responding on the I2C/SPI bus, causing motion sensing to fail and potentially triggering a kernel panic in the sensor stack.

probabilities.description: Impact damage dislodged or cracked the accelerometer IC.

probabilities.cause: I2C bus fault / solder joint failure
probabilities.description: I2C bus solder joint under accelerometer failed.
Power cycle corrupted IC configuration registers.

repair_steps: Test rotation/auto-rotate functionality
Test motion in Compass app — accelerometer drives it
Board-level: accelerometer IC replacement under microscope
test_procedure: Open Compass app — should show level indicator responding to tilt
Open a game using tilt controls — verify response

patterns.title: Gyroscope sensor failure
patterns.diagnosis: The gyroscope sensor (often co-located with accelerometer in an IMU chip) failed or is providing invalid data, affecting AR, gaming, and image stabilisation.

probabilities.description: Physical damage to 6-axis IMU chip damaging gyro MEMS structure.
Dust or moisture entering MEMS structure causing drift/failure.
Gyro factory calibration NVM data corrupted.

repair_steps: Test gyro in ARKit app or Compass app (navigation)
Restore iPhone to reset gyro calibration data
If hardware: IMU chip replacement
test_procedure: Open Compass app — walk in a circle, heading should update smoothly
Test AR app (Measure app) for tracking stability

patterns.title: Magnetometer / Compass sensor failure
patterns.diagnosis: The magnetometer (compass sensor, typically AKM AK09911) failed or is severely miscalibrated, causing the Compass app and magnetic heading in Maps to be unavailable.

probabilities.cause: Magnetometer IC damaged (often near magnetic accessories)
probabilities.description: Strong magnets (cases, mounts) permanently magnetised or damaged the sensor.
I2C communication failure between magnetometer and AP.
NVM calibration data corrupted, requiring factory reset to recalibrate.

repair_steps: Calibrate compass: Settings > Privacy > Location Services > Compass Calibration > On
Move away from magnetic cases/accessories and recalibrate
If still failing: magnetometer IC replacement
test_procedure: Open Compass app — should show heading and prompt calibration if needed
Rotate phone through figure-8 motion to calibrate

patterns.title: LiDAR scanner (ToF depth sensor) failure
patterns.diagnosis: The LiDAR scanner (SPAD-based time-of-flight depth sensor on Pro iPhones) failed or is not responding, breaking ARKit scene understanding and portrait lighting.

probabilities.cause: LiDAR module damaged by drop (rear camera impact)
probabilities.description: Physical impact to rear camera module damaged LiDAR SPAD array.
LiDAR module flex cable connection broken or loose.
IR VCSEL laser emitter in LiDAR module has failed.

repair_steps: Test ARKit app (Measure) — LiDAR required for instant AR
Rear camera module assembly replacement (LiDAR is part of camera module)
test_procedure: Open Measure app — tap '+' to measure; should work instantly with LiDAR
Open Instagram filters that use face/AR — depth-dependent effects should work

patterns.title: Barometric pressure sensor failure
patterns.diagnosis: The barometric pressure sensor (used for floor-level detection and weather) is missing from sensor reports or providing invalid data, causing thermal/altitude monitoring failures.

probabilities.description: LPS22 or BMP280 IC failed — common after liquid damage.
Tiny barometer vent hole on device sealed by adhesive or case.
I2C communication to barometer chip interrupted.

repair_steps: Check baro vent (usually near SIM tray or bottom of device)
Unblock vent with fine needle if debris-blocked
If IC failed: replacement requires board-level repair
test_procedure: Download a barometric pressure app to test sensor reading
Compare reading to local weather service pressure reading

patterns.title: Taptic Engine / haptic actuator failure
patterns.diagnosis: The Taptic Engine (linear resonant actuator) failed or is producing abnormal haptic patterns. Can cause kernel panics if the haptic driver detects a fault condition.

probabilities.description: Linear actuator mass displaced or coil damaged by impact.
Cable to Taptic Engine damaged or connector loose.
DRV2624 or equivalent haptic driver IC failed.

repair_steps: Test in Settings > Sounds & Haptics — Haptic Preview slider
Taptic Engine replacement (standalone or with battery assembly depending on model)
test_procedure: Settings > Sounds & Haptics > System Haptics — toggle on/off and feel
3D Touch / Haptic Touch long-press on app icon

patterns.title: STK3310/STK3335 ALS+Proximity IC failure
patterns.diagnosis: The Sensortek STK3310 or STK3335 combined ambient light and proximity sensor IC is not responding, causing both auto-brightness and screen-off-during-call to fail.

probabilities.description: Sensor IC on front flex assembly damaged.

probabilities.cause: Front panel flex cable connector fault
probabilities.description: Connector carrying ALS/prox signals to logic board loose or broken.
Aftermarket screen has opaque material over sensor window.

repair_steps: Check auto-brightness (Settings > Accessibility > Display > Auto-Brightness)
Test proximity: call yourself, put phone to ear — screen should go dark
Front assembly replacement if IC confirmed failed
test_procedure: Cover the top front sensor area during a call — screen should stay off
Settings > Accessibility > Display & Text Size > Auto-Brightness — toggle and test

patterns.title: ARKit session crash / AR framework fault
patterns.diagnosis: An ARKit session crashed, either due to hardware sensor unavailability (camera, LiDAR, IMU) or a software bug in the AR framework or app.

probabilities.description: App's camera access revoked while AR session was running.
LiDAR or camera not functioning, ARSession fails to initialise.

probabilities.cause: ARKit software crash in iOS version
probabilities.description: Known ARKit bug fixed in later iOS point release.

repair_steps: Grant camera permission: Settings > Privacy > Camera
Update iOS and the AR app
If camera fails: camera hardware inspection
test_procedure: Test in Measure app — basic ARKit without LiDAR
Check camera permission in Settings > Privacy > Camera for affected app

patterns.title: Touch IC (Meson/Azalea/Rumba) failure or panic
patterns.diagnosis: Apple's custom touch controller (codenames Meson, Azalea, Rumba depending on generation) encountered a firmware crash or hardware fault, causing touch input to stop working.

probabilities.description: Cracked or hard-impact display damaged the touch IC on the screen assembly.

probabilities.cause: Touch IC firmware fault — iOS update required
probabilities.description: Touch IC firmware bug exposed by iOS version.
SPI bus between touch IC and AP experiencing errors.

repair_steps: Test touchscreen: open a note and draw across entire screen
If areas unresponsive: screen assembly replacement
Update iOS — touch IC firmware updates included
test_procedure: Draw diagonals in Notes app across full screen
Test all four corners and edges for responsiveness

patterns.title: Ultra Wideband (U1) chip fault
patterns.diagnosis: The Apple U1 ultra-wideband chip experienced a hardware or firmware fault, disabling precision FindMy direction, directional AirDrop, and CarKey functionality.

probabilities.description: U1 silicon failure — may be related to impact damage.
UWB antenna flex connection broken.

probabilities.cause: U1 firmware bug — iOS fix available
probabilities.description: U1 firmware issue resolved in later iOS version.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Test Precision Finding in Find My app with another U1 device nearby
Apple service if U1 hardware confirmed failed
test_procedure: Use Find My app with another iPhone 11+ nearby — Precision Finding arrow should appear
Test directional AirDrop with another U1 iPhone

patterns.title: Pointer Authentication Code (PAC) failure — ARM64e integrity violation
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64e PAC (Pointer Authentication Code) check failed. A function pointer or return address had an invalid auth signature, indicating memory corruption or a code-reuse attack blocked by hardware.

probabilities.description: Heap or stack corruption overwrote a PAC-signed pointer, failing re-auth on use.
Driver compiled without PAC calling into PAC-protected kernel code.
PAC hardware blocked a code-reuse attack vector.

repair_steps: Update iOS — PAC violations in system code are fixed by iOS updates
DFU restore if persistent
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/arm64/sleh.c — EXC_ARM_PAC_FAIL exception code
Check iOS version for security patches

patterns.title: Kernel BTI failure — Branch Target Identification violation
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 BTI (Branch Target Identification) hardware protection blocked an indirect branch to a non-BTI-landing-pad address. Indicates control-flow hijack attempt or kernel bug.

probabilities.cause: Control flow hijack blocked by BTI
probabilities.description: Attempted indirect branch to non-landing-pad instruction — security protection triggered.

probabilities.cause: Kernel driver not compiled with BTI
probabilities.description: KEXT or driver object code without BTI landing pads called from BTI-enforced kernel.
JIT-compiled code missing BTI landing pad instructions.

repair_steps: Update iOS
DFU restore if persistent
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/arm64/sleh.c — 'Kernel BTI failure (BTYPE=0x%04x)'
Update iOS for kernel security fixes

patterns.title: Kernel integrity violation — KTRR/PPL protection triggered
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel Integrity Protection (KIP/KTRR) detected an attempt to modify protected kernel memory. The kernel text region is locked at boot — any write attempt causes an immediate panic.

probabilities.cause: Kernel driver attempted to write to kernel text
probabilities.description: Buggy driver wrote to read-only kernel code region.
Heap corruption propagated to protected kernel memory region.
Kernel Patch Protection blocked unauthorized kernel modification.

repair_steps: Update iOS — kernel integrity violations fixed by security updates
DFU restore
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/arm64/sleh.c — 'Kernel integrity, violation in frame 0x%016lx'
DFU restore to latest iOS

patterns.title: Invalid kernel stack pointer — stack overflow or corruption
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 exception handler detected an invalid kernel SP (stack pointer). Either the kernel stack overflowed its guard page, or the SP register was corrupted by a memory safety violation.

probabilities.description: Deep call chain or recursion in kernel driver exhausted the kernel thread stack.

probabilities.cause: Stack pointer corruption from memory error
probabilities.description: Memory corruption (DRAM bit flip or use-after-free) overwrote the SP.
Boot-time stack not yet initialized — usually a one-time occurrence.

repair_steps: Update iOS
DFU restore if persistent
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/arm64/sleh.c — exact string 'Invalid kernel stack pointer (probable overflow).'

patterns.title: Kernel parity error — DRAM uncorrectable memory error
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 exception handler reported a kernel parity error. On Apple Silicon, this indicates an uncorrectable DRAM ECC error — a hardware memory failure.

probabilities.description: LPDDR DRAM cell failure producing uncorrectable bit error detected by ECC.
Memory controller hardware error generating false parity signal.
DRAM Package-on-Package solder connection causing intermittent parity errors.

repair_steps: Logic board replacement required — DRAM is PoP soldered to SoC
If intermittent: may coincide with device overheating or power issues
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/arm64/sleh.c — 'Kernel parity error.'
Run memory stress test — does it reproduce under thermal load?

patterns.title: SP alignment exception from kernel — stack pointer misaligned
patterns.diagnosis: ARM64 requires the stack pointer to be 16-byte aligned at function calls. A kernel driver violated this constraint, causing an immediate alignment exception.

probabilities.cause: Kernel driver assembly with wrong SP alignment
probabilities.description: Hand-written assembly in driver not maintaining 16-byte SP alignment.
Buffer overflow corrupted SP to a misaligned value.
Incorrect codegen producing misaligned SP in function prologue.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Remove recently installed profiles/MDM configs
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/arm64/sleh.c — 'SP alignment exception from kernel.'

patterns.title: Zone metadata corruption — kernel heap integrity failure
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel zone allocator detected corrupted zone metadata. Zone metadata tracks allocation state — corruption indicates a use-after-free, double-free, or out-of-bounds write in kernel code.

probabilities.description: Freed zone element reused by kernel, overwriting zone's free-list metadata.

probabilities.cause: Out-of-bounds write from adjacent zone element
probabilities.description: Buffer overrun from one allocation corrupting neighboring zone metadata.

probabilities.cause: DRAM bit error in zone metadata region
probabilities.description: Hardware memory error flipping bit in zone management structures.

repair_steps: Update iOS — zone corruption bugs are fixed in kernel security patches
DFU restore if persistent
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/kern/zalloc.c — 'zone metadata corruption: %s (meta %p, zone %s%s)'
Update iOS to latest version

patterns.title: Zone allocator double-free — kernel memory freed twice
patterns.diagnosis: Zone allocator detected the same memory element being freed twice. Exact string from XNU zalloc.c: '%s: double free of %p to zone %s%s'

probabilities.description: IOKit object released more times than retained — zone catches the double-free.
Concurrent teardown paths both call free on the same object.

probabilities.cause: Corrupted reference count from DRAM error
probabilities.description: Memory error lowered refcount causing premature free.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Remove recently connected accessories
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/kern/zalloc.c — '%s: double free of %p to zone %s%s'

patterns.title: initproc (launchd) exited — complete system failure
patterns.diagnosis: launchd (pid 1, initproc) exited or failed to start. This is a fatal kernel panic — without launchd, no userspace can function. Indicates severe iOS corruption or hardware failure.

probabilities.description: launchd binary or its dependencies corrupted on NAND.

probabilities.cause: NAND storage failure preventing launchd exec
probabilities.description: Flash storage too degraded for launchd to execute correctly.
Corrupted launchd configuration causing immediate exit.

repair_steps: DFU restore required — this is unrecoverable without restore
If DFU fails with 4013/4014: NAND hardware failure — logic board repair needed
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_exit.c — exact string 'initproc exited'
Attempt DFU restore immediately

patterns.title: Text page corruption — executable code integrity failure
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel detected corruption in a process's executable text pages. Source: kern_exit.c 'Text page corruption detected in dying process %d'. Indicates NAND flash error or DRAM error corrupting code pages.

probabilities.cause: NAND bad block in app code segment
probabilities.description: Flash storage error returning bad data for app's executable pages.

probabilities.cause: DRAM bit error in code cache
probabilities.description: DRAM error corrupting instruction cache content.
Kernel killed process because code pages modified (jailbreak or corruption).

repair_steps: Delete and reinstall affected app
DFU restore if system binary affected
Logic board inspection if DRAM/NAND suspected
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_exit.c — 'Text page corruption detected in dying process %d'
Reinstall app — recurs after reinstall = hardware

patterns.title: Jetsam: process thrashing — repeated rapid memory kills
patterns.diagnosis: iOS detected process thrashing: a process being killed and relaunched repeatedly in rapid succession due to memory pressure. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'proc-thrashing'.

probabilities.cause: App repeatedly relaunching into memory pressure
probabilities.description: App killed by jetsam, relaunches immediately, gets killed again in a tight loop.

probabilities.cause: Daemon restart loop under memory pressure
probabilities.description: System daemon restarted by launchd into persistent memory pressure.

probabilities.cause: Memory leak on each launch cycle
probabilities.description: App allocates large memory each launch before being killed again.

repair_steps: Restart device to clear memory pressure
Update app causing the thrash loop
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledProcThrashing → 'proc-thrashing'
Check for rapidly growing jetsam log entries for same process

patterns.title: Jetsam: file cache thrashing — page cache pressure
patterns.diagnosis: iOS file cache (unified buffer cache) thrashing: pages being evicted and re-loaded repeatedly. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'fc-thrashing'.

probabilities.cause: Working set too large for available RAM
probabilities.description: App's file access pattern exceeds available page cache, causing constant eviction/reload.
Concurrent large file I/O operations competing for page cache.

probabilities.cause: Memory-mapped files with random access pattern
probabilities.description: mmap'd file with random access causing excessive page faults.

repair_steps: Restart device
Close memory-intensive apps doing large file operations
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledFCThrashing → 'fc-thrashing'

patterns.title: Jetsam: vnode limit — kernel vnode table exhausted
patterns.diagnosis: iOS vnode table (kernel structure for open files/directories) exhausted. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'vnode-limit'. iOS kills processes to reclaim vnodes.

probabilities.cause: App or daemon with too many open files
probabilities.description: Process opened many files/directories without closing them.

probabilities.cause: File descriptor leak across many processes
probabilities.description: Multiple apps each leaking vnodes until system limit reached.
Spotlight re-indexing using many vnodes simultaneously.

repair_steps: Restart device to clear vnode table
Update app that may be leaking file descriptors
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledVnodes → 'vnode-limit'
Restart device resolves

patterns.title: Jetsam: disk space shortage — storage critically low
patterns.diagnosis: iOS killed processes because internal storage is critically low. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'disk-space-shortage'. Below ~500MB free, iOS aggressively kills processes.

probabilities.description: Less than 500MB free — iOS must kill processes to create space for system operations.
App caches consuming the last free storage.
System logs filling up last available storage.

repair_steps: Free up storage immediately: delete unused apps, photos, large files
Settings > General > iPhone Storage — use recommendations
Offload unused apps
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledDiskSpaceShortage → 'disk-space-shortage'
Settings > General > iPhone Storage — check free space

patterns.title: Jetsam: VM pageout starvation — memory reclaim system overloaded
patterns.diagnosis: VM pageout daemon is starved — can't reclaim pages fast enough to keep up with allocation rate. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'vm-pageout-starvation'.

probabilities.cause: Memory allocation rate exceeds pageout rate
probabilities.description: Apps allocating memory faster than the pageout daemon can compress/evict.
Degraded NAND causing pageout operations to be slow, starving the system.
High-priority allocation blocking pageout thread execution.

repair_steps: Restart device
Close CPU/memory-intensive apps
If NAND degraded: DFU restore or hardware repair
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledVMPageoutStarvation → 'vm-pageout-starvation'

patterns.title: Jetsam: long-idle-exit — app idle for extended period
patterns.diagnosis: Extended idle exit: process idle for longer than the long-idle threshold. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'long-idle-exit'. More aggressive than regular idle-exit.

probabilities.cause: Background app idle for hours without activity
probabilities.description: Normal iOS housekeeping — long-idle processes reclaimed after extended inactivity.
System daemon finished its work and left idle in memory.

repair_steps: No action needed — expected iOS behavior
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledLongIdleExit → 'long-idle-exit'

patterns.title: Jetsam: conclave memory limit — App Intents / extensibility memory
patterns.diagnosis: App Conclave (iOS 17+ extensibility isolation container) exceeded its memory limit. Source: kern_memorystatus.c kill reason 'conclave-limit'. Affects App Intents, Live Activities, and Widgets.

probabilities.cause: App Intent / Live Activity memory overuse
probabilities.description: App Intents or Live Activity extension using too much memory in its isolated container.
Home Screen widget exceeding its memory budget in the conclave.
Memory leak inside the extensibility conclave container.

repair_steps: Update app — developer needs to reduce extension memory footprint
Remove Live Activity if problematic
test_procedure: Source: bsd/kern/kern_memorystatus.c — kMemorystatusKilledConclaveLimit → 'conclave-limit'
Only on iOS 17+

patterns.title: EXC_RESOURCE: wakeup rate exceeded — 150 wakeups/sec limit
patterns.diagnosis: App exceeded the system-enforced wakeup rate limit of 150 wakeups/second over a 300-second window. Source: XNU task.c TASK_WAKEUPS_MONITOR_DEFAULT_LIMIT = 150.

probabilities.description: App using timers firing more than 150 times/second continuously.

probabilities.cause: Tight IPC loop causing excessive wakeups
probabilities.description: Mach message send/receive loop generating rapid thread wakeups.
Busy-wait loop disguised as async callbacks generating wakeups.

repair_steps: Update app
Report to developer: reduce timer frequency or use coalesced timers
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/kern/task.c — TASK_WAKEUPS_MONITOR_DEFAULT_LIMIT = 150/sec over 300s
EXC_RESOURCE type WAKEUPS in crash log

keywords: possibly it still holds a rwlock
patterns.title: Kernel RWLock not released at thread termination
patterns.diagnosis: Kernel thread terminated while still holding an RW lock. Source: osfmk/kern/thread.c — 'rwlock_count is %d for thread %p, possibly it still holds a rwlock'.

probabilities.cause: Kernel driver exiting lock without releasing RWLock
probabilities.description: Driver error path returning without calling lck_rw_unlock.
Kernel fault inside a locked section prevented unlock.
Driver terminated (thread killed) while lock was held.

repair_steps: Update iOS — usually fixed in iOS point releases
Update or remove third-party app triggering the driver path
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/kern/thread.c — 'rwlock_count is %d for thread %p, possibly it still holds a rwlock'

patterns.title: IPC message queue panic — Mach messaging system corruption
patterns.diagnosis: Mach IPC message queue encountered an unexpected internal state. Source: osfmk/ipc/ipc_mqueue.c. Indicates kernel IPC subsystem corruption or invariant violation.

probabilities.description: IPC thread state corrupted, causing message queue to reach impossible state.
Wrong type of wait queue passed to IPC receive — driver or kernel bug.
Heap corruption overwriting IPC queue structures.

repair_steps: Update iOS
Restart device
test_procedure: Source: osfmk/ipc/ipc_mqueue.c — 'ipc_mqueue_receive_results: strange ith_state %d'
